definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point c if and only if (i) f is defined at c, (ii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists, and (iii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c equals f(c).
conditions: f is defined at c
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists
The limit equals f(c)
counterexample: Consider the function f defined on the real line by f(x) = sin(1/x) for x not equal to 0 and f(0) = 0. At x = 0, f is defined (condition i is met) and f(0) = 0. But the limit of sin(1/x) as x approaches 0 does not exist, because sin(1/x) oscillates between -1 and 1 with increasing rapidity. Hence condition (ii) fails, and the definition correctly identifies f as discontinuous at 0. Now consider a more subtle variant: g(x) = x sin(1/x) for x not equal to 0 and g(0) = 0. Here the limit as x approaches 0 does exist and equals 0, so all three conditions are met and g is continuous at 0. Yet g exhibits infinitely wild oscillation near the origin and is not differentiable there. The definition captures continuity but cannot distinguish between well-behaved and pathologically oscillating continuous functions.
missing_condition: The definition correctly characterises continuity at a point but provides no means of distinguishing between degrees or qualities of continuity. Functions that are continuous in the epsilon-delta sense may nonetheless exhibit behaviour — such as being nowhere differentiable — that the naive concept of continuity was not designed to accommodate. A stronger notion, such as uniform continuity or Lipschitz continuity, would be needed.
passage: The standard definition of continuity at a point is well known: a function is continuous at c if and only if it is defined at c, the limit as x approaches c exists, and the limit equals f(c). This definition, which formalises the intuitive idea that a continuous function has no jumps or gaps, is one of the triumphs of nineteenth-century analysis. It replaced vague appeals to functions that can be drawn without lifting the pen from the paper with a precise condition expressible in the language of limits. Yet one might observe that the definition, though it admirably captures what it sets out to capture, admits functions whose behaviour near the point of continuity is, by any intuitive standard, wild. Consider the function g(x) = x sin(1/x) for x not equal to zero, with g(0) = 0. This function is continuous at the origin by the three conditions: it is defined there, the limit exists and equals zero, and the function value is zero. Yet in any neighbourhood of the origin, no matter how small, the function oscillates infinitely many times between positive and negative values, and it fails to be differentiable at the origin. The three conditions are satisfied, but the function's behaviour bears little resemblance to the smooth, well-behaved curves that originally motivated the concept of continuity. The definition, it appears, captures a necessary condition for good behaviour but not a sufficient one. What is wanted, for many purposes, is a stronger notion — perhaps uniform continuity or a Lipschitz condition — that excludes the pathological oscillations the bare definition permits.

definition: An infinite series a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + ... converges if and only if (i) the sequence of partial sums S_n = a_1 + a_2 + ... + a_n has a finite limit L as n tends to infinity, (ii) for every epsilon greater than 0 there exists N such that |S_n - L| < epsilon for all n > N, and (iii) the terms a_n tend to 0 as n tends to infinity.
conditions: The partial sums S_n have a finite limit L
For every epsilon > 0 there exists N with |S_n - L| < epsilon for all n > N
The terms a_n tend to 0
counterexample: Consider the harmonic series 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 + .... The terms 1/n tend to 0 as n tends to infinity, so condition (iii) is satisfied. Yet the partial sums grow without bound — they diverge to infinity — so conditions (i) and (ii) are not met. Here the conditions are not jointly satisfied, and the definition correctly classifies the series as divergent. But this reveals that condition (iii) is not on equal footing with the others: conditions (i) and (ii) are equivalent reformulations of convergence, while condition (iii) is merely a necessary condition that is not sufficient. The definition presents a necessary condition as if it were a co-equal part of a sufficient set of conditions.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is not an independent condition but a consequence of conditions (i) and (ii). Its inclusion creates the misleading impression that it adds something to the definition, when in fact any series satisfying (i) and (ii) automatically satisfies (iii). The definition would be improved by noting that (iii) is a necessary but not sufficient condition, and that the sufficiency lies entirely in (i) and (ii).
passage: One might define convergence of an infinite series as follows: the series converges if and only if the partial sums have a finite limit, the partial sums eventually stay within any specified distance of that limit, and the individual terms tend to zero. The first two conditions are, in the language of analysis, equivalent formulations of the same requirement — they both express the epsilon-delta characterisation of a limit. The third condition captures a necessary condition that any student of calculus learns early: if a series converges, its terms must approach zero. The definition appears to provide three independent conditions for convergence. Yet one might observe that the three conditions are not on equal footing. Conditions (i) and (ii) are equivalent reformulations of convergence — each implies the other, and together they constitute the standard definition. Condition (iii), by contrast, is a consequence of (i) and (ii): if the partial sums converge, the terms must tend to zero. Including it in the definition creates the impression that it is an additional requirement, when in fact it is already guaranteed by the other two conditions. More importantly, condition (iii) is not sufficient for convergence. The harmonic series 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + ... has terms tending to zero, yet its partial sums diverge to infinity. Condition (iii) is satisfied, but the series does not converge. The definition, it appears, is misleadingly structured: it presents a consequence as though it were a co-equal condition, and the consequence, taken alone, would not suffice. What the definition lacks is a clear distinction between conditions that are jointly sufficient and conditions that are merely necessary.

definition: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X cannot be expressed as the union of two non-empty disjoint open sets, (ii) the only subsets of X that are both open and closed are X itself and the empty set, and (iii) every continuous function from X to the two-point discrete space {0, 1} is constant.
conditions: X cannot be expressed as the union of two non-empty disjoint open sets
The only clopen subsets of X are X and the empty set
Every continuous function from X to {0, 1} is constant
counterexample: Consider the topologist's sine curve: the graph of sin(1/x) for x in (0, 1] together with the segment {0} x [-1, 1] on the y-axis. This space is connected — it satisfies all three conditions — because any open set containing part of the curve and part of the segment must, owing to the accumulation of the curve near the y-axis, contain points from both. Yet the space is not path-connected: there is no continuous path from a point on the curve to a point on the vertical segment. The definition captures connectedness but cannot distinguish it from the stronger and often more useful notion of path-connectedness.
missing_condition: The definition correctly characterises topological connectedness but does not capture path-connectedness — the property that any two points can be joined by a continuous path. Many applications in analysis and geometry require path-connectedness, and the definition provides no means of detecting its absence.
passage: One might define a topological space to be connected if and only if it admits no partition into two non-empty disjoint open sets, its only clopen subsets are the whole space and the empty set, and every continuous function to the two-point discrete space is constant. These three conditions are, in fact, equivalent — each implies the others — and together they constitute the standard definition of connectedness. The definition captures the intuitive idea of a space that is all in one piece, with no gap or separation dividing it into disjoint parts. Yet one might observe that the definition, though it captures a genuine topological property, does not capture what is often the more useful property of path-connectedness. Consider the topologist's sine curve: the set consisting of the graph of sin(1/x) for x in the interval (0, 1] together with the vertical segment from (0, -1) to (0, 1). This space is connected by all three conditions, for the oscillations of the curve accumulate against the vertical segment in such a way that no separation into disjoint open sets is possible. Yet the space is not path-connected: there is no continuous path from a point on the oscillating curve to a point on the vertical segment. The three conditions are satisfied, but the space lacks a property — the ability to join any two of its points by a continuous path — that is essential for many purposes in analysis and geometry. The definition, it appears, captures a weaker notion than the one that many applications require.

definition: A function f from the natural numbers to the natural numbers is computable if and only if (i) there exists a finite set of instructions — an algorithm — that, given any input n, produces f(n) as output after finitely many steps, (ii) the algorithm requires no ingenuity or judgement on the part of the person executing it — each step is mechanically determined by the preceding step and the instructions, and (iii) the algorithm halts on every input.
conditions: There exists a finite algorithm that produces f(n) from n in finitely many steps
Each step is mechanically determined — no ingenuity required
The algorithm halts on every input
counterexample: Consider the function g defined by g(n) = 1 if the n-th Turing machine halts on input n, and g(n) = 0 otherwise. This function is well-defined — for each n, there is a definite fact about whether the n-th machine halts on input n. One might attempt to compute g by simulating the n-th machine on input n. If the machine halts, one outputs 1. The simulation is mechanical — each step is determined by the machine's transition function — and it is described by a finite set of instructions. But condition (iii) fails: if the n-th machine does not halt, the simulation runs forever and never produces output. The definition correctly excludes g as non-computable, but it does so by the halting condition, which raises the deeper question of which functions can be computed if we relax the requirement of totality — that is, if we permit partial functions that are defined only on those inputs where the algorithm halts.
missing_condition: The definition requires totality — that the algorithm halt on every input — but the theory of computation recognises a broader class of partial computable functions whose algorithms may fail to halt on some inputs. The definition does not accommodate this broader notion, which is essential for understanding the limits of computation.
passage: One might define a computable function as one for which there exists a finite algorithm that produces the function's value from any input in finitely many mechanically determined steps, halting on every input. This definition captures the core intuition of computability: a function is computable if there is a recipe, requiring no ingenuity, that mechanically grinds out the answer in finite time. The finitude of the instructions ensures that the algorithm can be written down; the mechanical character of each step ensures that execution requires no insight or creativity; and the halting condition ensures that the algorithm always terminates with an answer. Yet one might observe that the halting condition, though it ensures the computability of total functions, leaves out of account a large and important class of procedures. Consider the function g(n) that returns 1 if the n-th Turing machine halts on input n and 0 otherwise. The most natural approach to computing g is to simulate the n-th machine on input n and wait to see whether it halts. The simulation is mechanical in every respect — each step is determined by the machine's transition table — but it may never terminate. The definition correctly excludes g from the class of computable functions, yet in doing so it draws attention to a limitation: many natural computational procedures are partial — they compute a definite value on some inputs but fail to halt on others. The theory of computation recognises these partial computable functions as fundamental, and any account of computability that insists on totality misses an essential part of the picture.

definition: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function f from A to B that is injective — no two elements of A are mapped to the same element of B, (ii) f is surjective — every element of B is the image of some element of A, and (iii) f is constructively specifiable — one can describe the correspondence in finite terms.
conditions: There exists an injective function from A to B
The function is surjective
The function is constructively specifiable in finite terms
counterexample: Consider the set of real numbers and the set of points on a line segment of length 1. That these two sets have the same cardinality follows from the existence of a bijection — for example, the tangent function restricted to an appropriate interval. This bijection is injective and surjective, and it is constructively specifiable. Now consider the well-ordering theorem, which (assuming the axiom of choice) guarantees that every set can be well-ordered. Using the axiom of choice, one can establish bijections between sets without constructing the bijection explicitly. Such bijections satisfy conditions (i) and (ii) but fail condition (iii), since the axiom of choice asserts existence without providing a constructive specification. The definition would deny equal cardinality in cases where only a non-constructive bijection exists, which conflicts with standard set-theoretic practice.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a constructive requirement that is foreign to the standard set-theoretic notion of cardinality, which requires only the existence of a bijection, not its constructive specification. Removing condition (iii) would align the definition with standard usage; retaining it restricts the concept in ways that conflict with accepted mathematics.
passage: One might define two sets as having the same cardinality if and only if there exists a function between them that is injective, surjective, and constructively specifiable. The first two conditions capture the standard notion of a bijection — a one-to-one correspondence between the elements of the two sets. The third condition adds the requirement that the correspondence be describable in finite terms, which might seem a natural constraint: how can we claim that two sets are of the same size if we cannot describe any particular pairing of their elements? Yet one might observe that condition (iii) sits uneasily with standard mathematical practice. The axiom of choice, one of the central axioms of modern set theory, asserts the existence of functions — in particular, choice functions and well-orderings — without providing any constructive means of specifying them. Using the axiom of choice, one can establish that two sets have the same cardinality by proving the existence of a bijection whose particular form is never exhibited. The bijection is injective and surjective, but it is not constructively specifiable in any finite description. Condition (iii) would deny equal cardinality in such cases, placing the definition at odds with the set-theoretic framework within which cardinality is ordinarily studied. The definition, it appears, has imported a constructive requirement into a domain where the prevailing standards of existence are not constructive. What is wanted, if one intends to capture the standard notion, is the removal of condition (iii); if one intends a constructive notion, then the definition delimits a different concept than the standard one.

definition: An ordered field F is complete if and only if (i) every non-empty subset of F that is bounded above has a least upper bound in F, (ii) every Cauchy sequence in F converges to a limit in F, and (iii) F contains the rational numbers as a subfield.
conditions: Every non-empty bounded-above subset has a least upper bound in F
Every Cauchy sequence converges to a limit in F
F contains the rationals as a subfield
counterexample: Consider the field of formal Laurent series over the rationals with the standard valuation. This is an ordered field that contains the rationals as a subfield and in which every Cauchy sequence (with respect to the valuation metric) converges. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied. Whether condition (i) holds depends on the ordering and the interpretation of boundedness, but one can construct ordered non-archimedean extensions of the rationals that are Cauchy-complete and contain the rationals yet fail to be the real numbers. More simply, consider the p-adic numbers: the field of p-adic rationals is a completion of the rationals with respect to the p-adic metric, every Cauchy sequence converges, and the rationals are contained as a subfield, yet the p-adics are not an ordered field in the usual sense. The definition, by combining conditions that are equivalent only in the archimedean context, conflates properties that come apart in more general settings.
missing_condition: The definition omits the archimedean property — the requirement that for any element x of F there exists a natural number n with n > x. Without this condition, the definition cannot distinguish the real numbers from non-archimedean complete ordered fields, nor does it adequately separate the least-upper-bound property from Cauchy completeness in the general case.
passage: One might propose that an ordered field is complete if and only if every non-empty bounded-above subset has a least upper bound, every Cauchy sequence converges to a limit within the field, and the field contains the rationals as a subfield. The first condition is the least-upper-bound property, which characterises the real numbers among the ordered fields. The second is Cauchy completeness, which captures the idea that limits of convergent sequences do not escape the field. The third ensures that the field is an extension of the rationals. In the case of the real numbers, these three conditions are all satisfied, and conditions (i) and (ii) are in fact equivalent given the archimedean property. Yet one might observe that the definition conflates properties that are equivalent only in a particular context. The least-upper-bound property and Cauchy completeness are equivalent in archimedean ordered fields, but the definition does not require the archimedean property. One can construct non-archimedean ordered fields that contain the rationals and are Cauchy-complete in certain metrics yet fail to satisfy the least-upper-bound property, or vice versa. The definition bundles together conditions that come apart when the implicit assumption of the archimedean property is dropped. What is missing is an explicit statement of the archimedean condition — the requirement that for any element of the field there exists a natural number exceeding it — which is the assumption under which conditions (i) and (ii) are equivalent and under which the definition uniquely characterises the reals.

definition: A set S of natural numbers is decidable if and only if (i) there exists an algorithm that, given any natural number n, halts and outputs 'yes' if n is in S, (ii) the same algorithm halts and outputs 'no' if n is not in S, and (iii) the algorithm's running time is bounded by some computable function of n.
conditions: An algorithm halts and outputs 'yes' for every n in S
The same algorithm halts and outputs 'no' for every n not in S
The algorithm's running time is bounded by a computable function of n
counterexample: Consider the set of all n such that the n-th Turing machine eventually halts on input 0. A brilliant logician proves that there exists a decision procedure for this set — an algorithm satisfying conditions (i) and (ii) — but the proof is non-constructive and gives no information about how long the algorithm takes. Yet the running time, though finite for each input, may grow faster than any computable function. In the standard theory of computability, the set would be called decidable if conditions (i) and (ii) are met, regardless of the growth rate of the running time. Condition (iii) imposes an efficiency constraint that is foreign to the standard notion. Conversely, the set of prime numbers is decidable by a trial-division algorithm whose running time is bounded by a polynomial in n; condition (iii) is easily met, but it adds nothing to the classification of the set as decidable.
missing_condition: The definition conflates decidability with efficient decidability by imposing a running-time bound. The standard notion of decidability requires only that the algorithm halt on every input with the correct answer; no constraint on running time is imposed. Condition (iii) restricts the concept beyond what the standard notion demands.
passage: One might define a set of natural numbers as decidable if there exists an algorithm that halts on every input, outputting 'yes' for members of the set and 'no' for non-members, and whose running time is bounded by some computable function. Conditions (i) and (ii) capture the standard notion of decidability — also called recursive or computable — as it appears in the foundational work of Turing, Church, and others. The requirement of bounded running time, condition (iii), adds a constraint on the efficiency of the algorithm. This constraint might seem natural: an algorithm that takes an incomprehensibly long time on large inputs is, in practice, useless. Yet one might observe that condition (iii) is foreign to the standard definition of decidability, which requires only that the algorithm halt in finite time on every input, with no constraint on how rapidly the running time grows. The standard notion is purely existence-theoretic: a set is decidable if a decision procedure exists, regardless of its efficiency. By imposing a computable bound on running time, the definition excludes sets that are decidable in the standard sense but whose decision procedures have running times growing faster than any computable function. Such sets are, in the standard classification, decidable; the definition would reclassify them as undecidable, which conflicts with established usage. It appears, then, that the definition has conflated the notion of decidability with that of efficiently decidable — a notion closer to the complexity-theoretic concept of membership in a complexity class. What is wanted, for agreement with the standard notion, is the removal of condition (iii).

definition: A polynomial p(x) with coefficients in a field F is irreducible over F if and only if (i) the degree of p(x) is at least 1, (ii) p(x) cannot be factored as a product of two polynomials of positive degree with coefficients in F, and (iii) p(x) has no root in F.
conditions: The degree of p(x) is at least 1
p(x) cannot be factored into two positive-degree polynomials over F
p(x) has no root in F
counterexample: Consider the polynomial x^4 + 4 over the rational numbers. It has no rational root — one can verify that no rational number satisfies x^4 + 4 = 0 — so condition (iii) is met. Its degree is 4, satisfying condition (i). Yet x^4 + 4 factors as (x^2 + 2x + 2)(x^2 - 2x + 2) over the rationals, so condition (ii) is not satisfied. The polynomial is reducible despite having no root. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not equivalent for polynomials of degree greater than 3: the absence of roots guarantees irreducibility only for polynomials of degree 2 or 3.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is not equivalent to condition (ii) for polynomials of degree 4 or higher. A polynomial of degree at least 4 may have no root in F yet still factor into polynomials of lower degree. The definition creates the misleading impression that rootlessness is an independent, co-equal condition for irreducibility when in fact it is neither necessary nor sufficient in general.
passage: One might propose that a polynomial is irreducible over a field if and only if its degree is at least 1, it cannot be factored into two polynomials of positive degree over that field, and it has no root in the field. The first condition excludes constants; the second is the standard definition of irreducibility; and the third captures the intuition that an irreducible polynomial cannot be solved within the field. For polynomials of degree 2 or 3, conditions (ii) and (iii) are indeed equivalent: a quadratic or cubic polynomial over a field is reducible if and only if it has a root in that field, because any non-trivial factorisation of a polynomial of degree 2 or 3 must include a linear factor, which corresponds to a root. Yet one might observe that this equivalence breaks down for polynomials of higher degree. The polynomial x^4 + 4, considered over the rational numbers, has no rational root — one may verify this by substitution — and so condition (iii) is satisfied. But x^4 + 4 factors as the product of x^2 + 2x + 2 and x^2 - 2x + 2, both polynomials of positive degree with rational coefficients. Condition (ii) is violated. The polynomial is reducible despite being rootless. The definition presents conditions (ii) and (iii) as though they were independent co-equal requirements, when in fact (iii) is a consequence of (ii) only in low degree. For polynomials of degree 4 or higher, factorisation into two quadratics — neither of which need have a root — is perfectly possible. The definition, it appears, has been misled by the coincidence of rootlessness and irreducibility in the low-degree case.

definition: A sequence of functions f_1, f_2, f_3, ... converges to a function f on a domain D if and only if (i) for each point x in D, f_n(x) tends to f(x) as n tends to infinity, (ii) each f_n is continuous on D, and (iii) f is continuous on D.
conditions: For each x in D, f_n(x) tends to f(x)
Each f_n is continuous on D
f is continuous on D
counterexample: Consider the sequence of functions f_n(x) = x^n on the interval [0, 1]. Each f_n is continuous on [0, 1]. For each x in [0, 1), f_n(x) tends to 0 as n tends to infinity, and f_n(1) = 1 for all n. The pointwise limit function f is therefore f(x) = 0 for x in [0, 1) and f(1) = 1. But this limit function is not continuous on [0, 1] — it has a jump discontinuity at x = 1. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) fails. The definition includes continuity of the limit as a condition, but the real difficulty is that pointwise convergence of continuous functions does not guarantee continuity of the limit. The definition avoids the difficulty by fiat rather than by identifying the stronger mode of convergence — uniform convergence — that would ensure it.
missing_condition: The definition conflates two distinct issues: the mode of convergence (pointwise vs. uniform) and the properties of the limit. Condition (iii) is not a property of the convergence but of the limit function, and its failure is a symptom of the inadequacy of pointwise convergence. What is missing is a condition requiring uniform convergence, which would guarantee the continuity of the limit without needing to stipulate it separately.
passage: One might define convergence of a sequence of functions as follows: the sequence converges to a function f on a domain D if and only if each f_n(x) tends to f(x) for every x in D, each f_n is continuous, and f is continuous. This definition appears to combine the convergence condition with desirable properties of the functions involved. The first condition is pointwise convergence — the most natural and elementary mode of convergence for sequences of functions. The second and third conditions require continuity of the approximating functions and of the limit, which might seem to guarantee a well-behaved convergence. Yet one might observe that the definition has papered over a genuine difficulty rather than resolving it. Consider the sequence f_n(x) = x^n on [0, 1]. Each f_n is continuous, and for each x the values f_n(x) converge to a definite limit: 0 if x < 1, and 1 if x = 1. The pointwise limit function is discontinuous at x = 1. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied but condition (iii) is not, and the definition excludes this case. But the exclusion is achieved by stipulation rather than by identifying the root cause. The real issue is that pointwise convergence of continuous functions does not preserve continuity; what preserves continuity is uniform convergence. By including condition (iii) as a stipulation, the definition hides the deeper insight that the mode of convergence, not the properties of the limit, is what needs strengthening. A more illuminating definition would replace condition (iii) with a requirement of uniform convergence, from which continuity of the limit would follow as a theorem.

definition: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than 1, (ii) n is divisible only by 1 and by itself, and (iii) n is not expressible as a product of two natural numbers each strictly between 1 and n.
conditions: n is greater than 1
n is divisible only by 1 and by itself
n is not expressible as a product of two natural numbers each strictly between 1 and n
counterexample: Consider the number 1. It is not greater than 1, so it fails condition (i) and is correctly excluded. Now consider the integer -7 in the ring of integers. It is not a natural number, so the definition does not apply. But if one attempts to generalise the definition to all integers by replacing 'natural number' with 'integer' and adjusting accordingly, one finds that -7 is divisible only by 1, -1, 7, and -7, which complicates the notion of 'divisible only by 1 and by itself.' The definition as stated works for natural numbers but does not generalise to rings where the notion of 'unit' must replace the notion of '1.'
passage: One might define a prime number as a natural number that is greater than 1, is divisible only by 1 and by itself, and is not expressible as a product of two natural numbers each strictly between 1 and the number in question. This definition is, as far as it goes, perfectly adequate for the natural numbers. The first condition excludes 1, which is conventionally (and for good algebraic reasons) not regarded as prime. The second and third conditions are, for natural numbers, equivalent: a natural number greater than 1 that is divisible only by 1 and itself cannot be expressed as a product of two smaller natural numbers each exceeding 1, and conversely. The redundancy of the third condition is thus harmless in the present context. Yet one might observe that this definition, while correct within its intended domain, does not generalise to the broader algebraic setting in which the concept of primality plays its most important role. In ring theory, the notion of a prime element is defined not in terms of divisibility by 1 and the element itself, but in terms of the property that if the element divides a product, it must divide at least one of the factors. This is the distinction between being prime and being irreducible. In the natural numbers and in the integers, these two notions coincide, but in more general rings they may diverge. The definition given, though serviceable for elementary arithmetic, thus conceals a conceptual distinction that becomes crucial in the algebraic setting.

definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point c if and only if (i) f is defined at c, (ii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists, and (iii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c is equal to f(c).
conditions: f is defined at c
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c is equal to f(c)
counterexample: Consider the function f defined on all real numbers by f(x) = sin(1/x) for x not equal to 0, and f(0) = 0. At c = 0, f is defined — condition (i) is satisfied. But the limit of sin(1/x) as x approaches 0 does not exist, since sin(1/x) oscillates between -1 and 1 without settling. Thus condition (ii) fails, and the definition correctly classifies f as discontinuous at 0. The definition works well for standard real-valued functions but does not extend to functions between general topological spaces where limits in the sequential sense may not capture continuity.
passage: One might define a real-valued function f as continuous at a point c if and only if f is defined at c, the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists, and that limit equals f(c). This three-part definition, which one encounters in any introductory course in analysis, captures the intuitive idea that a function is continuous at a point when its values near that point are close to its value at the point itself. The first condition ensures that the function is not being evaluated at a point outside its domain. The second ensures that the behaviour of f near c is sufficiently regular to admit a limiting value. The third ties the two together, requiring that the limiting behaviour agree with the actual value. For functions from the reals to the reals, this definition is entirely standard and adequate. Yet one might observe that the definition is implicitly dependent upon the metric structure of the real line, since the notion of a limit as x approaches c presupposes a notion of distance. When one moves to the more general setting of topological spaces, the epsilon-delta formulation of limits is no longer available, and continuity must be characterised in terms of the pre-images of open sets. The present definition, though correct in its own domain, does not generalise without substantial reformulation, and the student who relies upon it exclusively may find himself unprepared for the topological perspective.

definition: An infinite series S = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + ... converges if and only if (i) the sequence of partial sums s_n = a_1 + a_2 + ... + a_n is well-defined for all n, (ii) the sequence of partial sums is bounded, and (iii) the terms a_n approach zero as n approaches infinity.
conditions: The sequence of partial sums s_n is well-defined for all n
The sequence of partial sums is bounded
The terms a_n approach zero as n approaches infinity
counterexample: Consider the harmonic series 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 + .... The partial sums are well-defined for all n, and the terms 1/n approach zero as n approaches infinity. Yet the sequence of partial sums is not bounded — it diverges to infinity. The series thus fails condition (ii). Now consider the alternating series 1 - 1 + 1 - 1 + ..., whose terms do not approach zero and whose partial sums oscillate between 0 and 1, hence are bounded. It satisfies (i) and (ii) but fails (iii), and indeed does not converge. The definition correctly handles these cases, but consider the series 1 - 1/2 + 1/3 - 1/4 + ..., which converges conditionally. Its partial sums are bounded and its terms approach zero, so it satisfies all three conditions. Yet boundedness of partial sums plus vanishing terms does not in general suffice for convergence; an additional condition — that the partial sums form a Cauchy sequence — is required.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the sequence of partial sums converge to a definite limit; boundedness of partial sums combined with vanishing terms does not guarantee convergence, as the partial sums might oscillate without settling.
passage: One might propose that an infinite series converges if and only if its partial sums are well-defined for all n, the sequence of partial sums is bounded, and the terms of the series approach zero. This definition has a certain initial plausibility: it combines a regularity condition (well-definedness), a finiteness condition (boundedness), and what is often called the 'necessary condition for convergence' (the vanishing of terms). The harmonic series provides an instructive test case: its terms approach zero, but its partial sums are unbounded, so it is correctly classified as divergent under condition (ii). Similarly, the series 1 - 1 + 1 - 1 + ..., whose terms do not approach zero, is correctly excluded by condition (iii). Yet one might construct a case which reveals that the three conditions are not jointly sufficient. Consider a series whose terms approach zero and whose partial sums, while bounded, oscillate in a manner that prevents them from approaching any definite limit. For example, one might arrange the terms of a series so that the partial sums visit 0 and 1 alternately, with the size of the oscillations diminishing but never vanishing. Such a series satisfies all three conditions, yet it does not converge in the standard sense, which requires the partial sums to approach a unique limit. What is missing is the requirement that the sequence of partial sums be a Cauchy sequence, or equivalently, that it converge to a definite real number.

definition: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X is non-empty, (ii) X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets, and (iii) every continuous function from X to a discrete two-point space is constant.
conditions: X is non-empty
X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets
Every continuous function from X to a discrete two-point space is constant
counterexample: Consider the topologist's sine curve: the set consisting of the graph of sin(1/x) for x in (0,1] together with the segment {0} x [-1,1] in the plane. This space is connected — it satisfies all three conditions. Yet it is not path-connected: there is no continuous path from a point on the segment to a point on the curve. The definition correctly classifies it as connected, but one might initially expect connected spaces to be path-connected, and this case reveals that the two notions diverge.
passage: One might define a topological space as connected if and only if it is non-empty, cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets, and every continuous function from the space to a discrete two-point space is constant. These three conditions are, for non-empty spaces, equivalent in pairs: the second and third are well known to be equivalent formulations of connectedness, and the first is included merely to exclude the trivial case of the empty set (whose connectedness is a matter of convention). The definition thus captures precisely the standard notion of topological connectedness. Yet one might observe that the definition admits spaces whose connectedness is of a somewhat pathological character. The topologist's sine curve — the union of the graph of sin(1/x) for x in (0,1] with the vertical segment from (0,-1) to (0,1) — provides a classic illustration. This space is connected in the sense of the definition: it cannot be partitioned into two disjoint non-empty open sets, and every continuous function from it to a two-point discrete space is constant. Yet it is not path-connected: there is no continuous path joining a point on the vertical segment to a point on the oscillating curve. This case reveals that connectedness, as defined, does not entail the stronger and perhaps more intuitive property of path-connectedness. The definition is thus correct but potentially misleading to those who associate connectedness with the ability to travel continuously from any point to any other.

definition: A function f from the natural numbers to the natural numbers is computable if and only if (i) there exists a Turing machine M that, given any input n, eventually halts, (ii) upon halting, M produces f(n) as its output, and (iii) M operates deterministically, following a fixed finite set of rules.
conditions: There exists a Turing machine M that, given any input n, eventually halts
Upon halting, M produces f(n) as its output
M operates deterministically, following a fixed finite set of rules
counterexample: Consider the busy beaver function BB(n), defined as the maximum number of 1s that an n-state Turing machine can write on an initially blank tape before halting. For each fixed n, BB(n) is a definite natural number, so BB is a well-defined function from natural numbers to natural numbers. Yet BB grows faster than any computable function, and no Turing machine computes it. The definition correctly excludes BB, but suppose one considers a function that is computable by a non-deterministic Turing machine. Such a function might satisfy a relaxed version of the conditions, yet the definition requires determinism in condition (iii), which is appropriate for the standard notion of computability.
passage: One might define a function from the natural numbers to the natural numbers as computable if and only if there exists a Turing machine that, given any input, eventually halts, produces the correct output upon halting, and operates deterministically according to a fixed finite set of rules. This definition captures the standard notion of Turing-computability. The halting condition ensures that the computation terminates for every input, yielding what is called a total computable function. The correctness condition ensures that the machine's output agrees with the function's value. And the determinism condition ensures that the computation proceeds unambiguously, with each configuration of the machine determining a unique successor configuration. One might observe that the definition could be criticised for excluding partial functions — functions that are defined on some but not all natural numbers — from the class of computable functions. In computability theory, one frequently wishes to speak of partially computable functions, for which the Turing machine halts on inputs in the function's domain but may run forever on inputs outside it. The present definition, by requiring halting on every input, restricts attention to total computable functions. This is a legitimate and indeed common convention, but the student should be aware that the broader notion of partial computability plays a central role in the theory, particularly in the proof of the unsolvability of the halting problem and in the construction of the arithmetic hierarchy.

definition: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function f from A to B, (ii) f is injective, meaning that distinct elements of A are mapped to distinct elements of B, and (iii) f is surjective, meaning that every element of B is the image of some element of A.
conditions: There exists a function f from A to B
f is injective, meaning that distinct elements of A are mapped to distinct elements of B
f is surjective, meaning that every element of B is the image of some element of A
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers N = {0, 1, 2, ...} and the set of even natural numbers E = {0, 2, 4, ...}. The function f(n) = 2n is a bijection from N to E: it is injective and surjective. So N and E have the same cardinality according to the definition. Yet E is a proper subset of N. This result, though correct, strikes many as paradoxical, and it reveals that the defined notion of 'same size' diverges from the intuitive notion that a proper part must be smaller than the whole.
passage: One might define two sets as having the same cardinality if and only if there exists a function from one to the other that is both injective and surjective — that is, a bijection. This definition, due in its essentials to Cantor, has proved extraordinarily fruitful and is now standard in set theory. The injectivity condition ensures that distinct elements of the first set are matched with distinct elements of the second, so that no element of the second set receives more than one partner. The surjectivity condition ensures that every element of the second set has a partner, so that none is left unmatched. Together, these conditions establish a one-to-one correspondence between the elements of the two sets. Yet one might observe that this definition yields results that are, upon first encounter, deeply counterintuitive. Consider the natural numbers and the even natural numbers. The function that sends each natural number n to 2n is plainly injective and surjective: distinct natural numbers are sent to distinct even numbers, and every even number is the image of some natural number. The two sets therefore have the same cardinality. Yet the even numbers form a proper subset of the natural numbers — every even number is a natural number, but not every natural number is even. The definition thus commits us to saying that a set and a proper subset of it may be of the same size, a conclusion that conflicts with the intuition, deeply rooted in our experience with finite collections, that the part must be smaller than the whole. This is not a defect in the definition but rather an indication that the notion of size, when applied to infinite sets, behaves differently than one might naively expect.

definition: A formal system S is complete if and only if (i) S is consistent, meaning that no contradiction is derivable within S, (ii) for every sentence P in the language of S, either P or its negation is derivable in S, and (iii) the axioms and rules of inference of S are effectively specifiable.
conditions: S is consistent, meaning that no contradiction is derivable within S
For every sentence P in the language of S, either P or its negation is derivable in S
The axioms and rules of inference of S are effectively specifiable
counterexample: Consider Peano Arithmetic (PA), a formal system for the natural numbers whose axioms and rules of inference are effectively specifiable. PA is widely believed to be consistent. Yet by Goedel's first incompleteness theorem, if PA is consistent, there exist sentences in its language that are neither provable nor refutable within PA. PA thus satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but fails condition (ii). The definition correctly identifies PA as incomplete, but it reveals that conditions (i) and (iii) together preclude condition (ii) for any sufficiently expressive system.
passage: One might define a formal system as complete if and only if it is consistent, every sentence in its language or that sentence's negation is derivable within it, and its axioms and rules of inference are effectively specifiable. This definition combines three conditions that are individually well-motivated. Consistency is a sine qua non: an inconsistent system derives every sentence and is therefore trivially complete in a degenerate sense. The derivability condition expresses what we mean by completeness in the semantic sense — that the system settles every question expressible in its language. And the effective specifiability of axioms and rules ensures that the system is the sort of thing that could, in principle, be implemented by a mechanical procedure. What is remarkable about this definition, and what constitutes one of the most profound discoveries of twentieth-century mathematics, is that for sufficiently expressive formal systems, the three conditions are jointly unsatisfiable. Goedel's first incompleteness theorem demonstrates that any consistent, effectively axiomatised system capable of expressing elementary arithmetic must contain sentences that are neither provable nor refutable within the system. The definition is thus perfectly coherent as a definition, but the class of systems satisfying all three conditions, when restricted to systems of sufficient expressive power, is empty. This does not render the definition defective; rather, it reveals a deep fact about the limits of formal methods.

definition: A set S of natural numbers is decidable if and only if (i) there exists an algorithm A that takes any natural number n as input, (ii) A terminates in a finite number of steps for every input n, and (iii) A correctly determines whether n is a member of S, outputting 'yes' if n is in S and 'no' otherwise.
conditions: There exists an algorithm A that takes any natural number n as input
A terminates in a finite number of steps for every input n
A correctly determines whether n is a member of S
counterexample: Consider the set H of natural numbers that encode Turing machines which halt on the empty input. For any given encoding n, the question of whether n is in H is perfectly well-defined. One might attempt to construct an algorithm that simulates the encoded Turing machine and outputs 'yes' if it halts. Such an algorithm satisfies condition (i) and is correct when it terminates. However, if the Turing machine does not halt, the simulation runs forever, violating condition (ii). The halting problem establishes that no algorithm satisfying all three conditions exists for H.
passage: One might define a set of natural numbers as decidable if and only if there exists an algorithm that takes any natural number as input, terminates in a finite number of steps for every input, and correctly determines whether the input is a member of the set. This definition captures the intuitive notion of a set whose membership question can be settled mechanically. The first condition ensures that the algorithm is applicable to every natural number. The second ensures that the algorithm does not run forever — that it produces an answer in every case. The third ensures that the answer is correct. For many sets of interest in number theory, such algorithms are readily available: the set of even numbers, the set of prime numbers, and the set of perfect squares are all decidable. Yet one might observe that there exist sets for which no such algorithm can exist. The most celebrated example is the set of encodings of Turing machines that halt on the empty input. For any particular encoding, the question of membership is perfectly well-defined, and one might attempt to settle it by simulating the encoded machine. But if the machine does not halt, the simulation will run forever, and no alternative strategy can compensate for this deficiency. Turing's theorem establishes that no algorithm satisfying all three conditions exists for this set. The definition is thus correct and illuminating, for it draws a sharp boundary between the mechanically tractable and the mechanically intractable.

definition: A graph G is connected if and only if (i) G contains at least one vertex, (ii) for every pair of distinct vertices u and v in G there exists a path from u to v, and (iii) G contains no isolated vertices — that is, every vertex is incident to at least one edge.
conditions: G contains at least one vertex
For every pair of distinct vertices u and v in G there exists a path from u to v
G contains no isolated vertices — every vertex is incident to at least one edge
counterexample: Consider the graph consisting of a single vertex and no edges. It contains at least one vertex, and the condition that every pair of distinct vertices be connected by a path is vacuously satisfied (since there is no pair of distinct vertices). However, the sole vertex is isolated — it is incident to no edges — so condition (iii) is violated. The graph is, by standard definition, connected, since the path condition is vacuously met. The inclusion of condition (iii) incorrectly excludes this trivially connected graph.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is not merely unnecessary but actively incorrect; it excludes the single-vertex graph, which is connected by the standard definition. The definition should be corrected by removing this condition rather than adding a new one.
passage: One might define a graph as connected if and only if it contains at least one vertex, every pair of distinct vertices is connected by a path, and every vertex is incident to at least one edge. The first two conditions capture the essential content of graph-theoretic connectedness: a connected graph is one in which any vertex can be reached from any other by traversing a sequence of edges. The third condition is added, one might suppose, to exclude graphs with isolated vertices — vertices that are disconnected from the rest of the graph. Yet one might observe that the third condition creates a difficulty in the trivial case. Consider a graph consisting of a single vertex and no edges. The first condition is satisfied: the graph contains at least one vertex. The second condition is vacuously satisfied: there are no pairs of distinct vertices, so the universal quantification over such pairs is trivially true. By the standard graph-theoretic definition, this graph is connected. Yet condition (iii) classifies the sole vertex as isolated, since it is incident to no edge, and the graph is therefore deemed disconnected. This is the wrong result. The single-vertex graph is the simplest example of a connected graph, and any adequate definition ought to classify it as such. The error lies in the inclusion of the third condition, which is not merely redundant but actively misleading. The path condition alone, together with non-emptiness, suffices to characterise connectedness; the prohibition on isolated vertices is both unnecessary and incorrect.

definition: An ordered field F is complete if and only if (i) F is Archimedean, meaning that for every element x of F there exists a natural number n greater than x, (ii) every Cauchy sequence in F converges to an element of F, and (iii) F contains the rational numbers as a subfield.
conditions: F is Archimedean
Every Cauchy sequence in F converges to an element of F
F contains the rational numbers as a subfield
counterexample: Consider the field of real algebraic numbers — those real numbers that are roots of polynomials with integer coefficients. This field is Archimedean, contains the rationals as a subfield, and every Cauchy sequence of algebraic numbers that converges to an algebraic number does so within the field. But the field is not complete in the standard sense, because there exist Cauchy sequences of algebraic numbers that converge to transcendental numbers (such as e or pi) which are not in the field. Condition (ii) is thus violated, and the definition correctly classifies this field as incomplete.
passage: One might define an ordered field as complete if and only if it is Archimedean, every Cauchy sequence in it converges to an element of the field, and it contains the rational numbers as a subfield. This definition is designed to characterise the real numbers up to isomorphism, and it succeeds in doing so, since the real numbers are the unique (up to isomorphism) complete Archimedean ordered field containing the rationals. The Archimedean condition excludes non-standard models containing infinitesimal elements. The Cauchy completeness condition ensures that the field has no 'gaps' — that every sequence whose terms become arbitrarily close to one another eventually converges to an element of the field. And the requirement that the rationals be a subfield anchors the field to the familiar number system. One might observe that condition (iii) is in fact redundant, since every Archimedean ordered field is isomorphic to a subfield of the reals and therefore contains a copy of the rationals. Its inclusion is thus harmless but unnecessary. A more instructive observation concerns the relationship between Cauchy completeness and the least upper bound property. In the context of Archimedean ordered fields, these two forms of completeness are equivalent: an Archimedean ordered field is Cauchy complete if and only if every non-empty subset that is bounded above has a least upper bound. This equivalence does not hold for non-Archimedean ordered fields, a subtlety that the present definition obscures by assuming the Archimedean property from the outset.

definition: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than 1, (ii) n has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself, and (iii) n is odd.
conditions: n is greater than 1
n has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself
n is odd
counterexample: The number 2 is greater than 1 and has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself, yet it is even, not odd. The third condition fails, yet 2 is universally recognised as a prime number — indeed, it is the smallest prime. The definition excludes the only even prime.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously includes oddness as a necessary condition, which is not part of the standard characterisation of primality; the condition should be removed rather than replaced.
passage: One might propose that a natural number is prime if and only if it exceeds 1, possesses no positive divisors other than 1 and itself, and is odd. The first two conditions capture the standard characterisation, and the third might seem an innocuous addition, since the overwhelming majority of primes are indeed odd. Among the first hundred primes, for instance, all but one are odd, and in any sufficiently large interval the density of even primes is zero. Yet this seemingly harmless addition introduces an error that a single case suffices to reveal. The number 2 satisfies the first two conditions — it exceeds 1, and its only positive divisors are 1 and 2 — but it is even, failing the third condition. No mathematician would deny that 2 is prime; it is the unique even prime, a fact of considerable importance in number theory. The addition of the oddness requirement, though it correctly describes nearly all primes, is a case of mistaking a near-universal property for a necessary one. The definition inadvertently excludes the very number whose primality is most singular and most consequential for the structure of the integers.

definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point c if and only if (i) f is defined at c, (ii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists, and (iii) the value of f at c equals the limit of f(x) as x approaches c from the right.
conditions: f is defined at c
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists
f(c) equals the limit of f(x) as x approaches c from the right
counterexample: Consider the function defined by f(x) = 0 for x < 0 and f(x) = 1 for x >= 0. At c = 0, f is defined and equals 1. The left-hand limit is 0 and the right-hand limit is 1, so the full two-sided limit does not exist — but set that aside. The right-hand limit is 1, which equals f(0). The third condition is satisfied, yet the function has a jump discontinuity at 0. The definition's use of only the right-hand limit rather than the two-sided limit permits it to classify discontinuous functions as continuous.
missing_condition: The definition should require that f(c) equal the two-sided limit of f(x) as x approaches c, not merely the right-hand limit, so that continuity demands agreement from both directions.
passage: One might define continuity of a real-valued function at a point c as requiring that f be defined at c, that the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exist, and that f(c) equal the limit of f(x) as x approaches c from the right. The first two conditions are standard, and the third might seem merely a restatement of the usual epsilon-delta characterisation. Yet the third condition, by specifying the right-hand limit rather than the full two-sided limit, introduces a subtle asymmetry. Consider the step function that assigns the value 0 to every negative real number and the value 1 to every non-negative real number. At c = 0, the function is defined and equals 1. The right-hand limit is also 1, so the third condition is met. Yet the function has a manifest jump discontinuity at 0 — the left-hand limit is 0, not 1. The two-sided limit does not exist, and the second condition, properly interpreted, fails. But if one reads the second condition loosely, or if one focuses on the third condition alone, the definition misleadingly suggests that the function is continuous at 0. The correct requirement is that f(c) equal the two-sided limit, demanding agreement from both the left and the right, and the definition's restriction to the right-hand limit is a case which reveals the insufficiency of the proposed characterisation.

definition: An infinite series a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + ... converges if and only if (i) the terms a_n tend to zero as n tends to infinity, (ii) the partial sums S_n = a_1 + ... + a_n are bounded, and (iii) the partial sums are monotonically increasing.
conditions: The terms a_n tend to zero
The partial sums are bounded
The partial sums are monotonically increasing
counterexample: Consider the alternating series 1 - 1/2 + 1/3 - 1/4 + 1/5 - ... . The terms tend to zero, the partial sums are bounded (they oscillate between approximately 0.69 and 1), and the series converges to the natural logarithm of 2. Yet the partial sums are not monotonically increasing — every other partial sum is smaller than its predecessor. The third condition fails, notwithstanding that the series converges.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a monotonicity requirement that excludes convergent series with oscillating partial sums, such as conditionally convergent alternating series.
passage: One might propose that an infinite series converges if and only if its terms tend to zero, its partial sums are bounded, and the partial sums are monotonically increasing. The first condition is necessary for convergence (though not sufficient), the second captures the intuition that the partial sums must not escape to infinity, and the third might seem to ensure that the sums are settling toward a definite value. This characterisation works admirably for series of positive terms: if the partial sums of a positive series are bounded and increasing, the series converges by the monotone convergence theorem. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the alternating harmonic series 1 - 1/2 + 1/3 - 1/4 + ... . The terms tend to zero, and the partial sums are bounded — they oscillate within a narrow band converging on the natural logarithm of 2. The series converges, as the alternating series test confirms. But the partial sums are emphatically not monotonically increasing: each even partial sum is less than the preceding odd one. The third condition fails for this perfectly convergent series. The definition, by insisting on monotonicity, has mistaken a property of the special case — convergent positive series — for a property of convergent series in general, thereby excluding the large and important class of conditionally convergent series with oscillating partial sums.

definition: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X is non-empty, (ii) X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets, and (iii) every continuous function from X to the real numbers maps X onto an interval.
conditions: X is non-empty
X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets
Every continuous function from X to the reals maps X onto an interval
counterexample: The third condition is actually a consequence of the first two — it is the intermediate value property and it follows from connectedness. One might therefore suppose the definition is merely redundant. But consider the converse direction: the topologist's sine curve, consisting of the graph of sin(1/x) for x > 0 together with the segment {0} x [-1, 1], is connected and satisfies all three conditions. Yet the space is not path-connected — there is no continuous path from a point on the segment to a point on the curve. The definition characterises connectedness correctly but is sometimes confused with path-connectedness, and the redundant third condition encourages this confusion.
missing_condition: The third condition is not an independent requirement but a theorem that follows from conditions (i) and (ii); its inclusion renders the definition redundant rather than wrong, but it may encourage the mistaken inference that connectedness entails path-connectedness.
passage: One might define a topological space as connected if and only if it is non-empty, cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets, and has the property that every continuous real-valued function on it maps it onto an interval. The first two conditions constitute the standard definition of connectedness, and the third captures the intermediate value property that connected spaces enjoy. The three conditions are all true of connected spaces, but the inclusion of the third as an independent condition is misleading, for it is in fact a theorem that follows from the first two. The definition is not wrong, but it is redundant in a way that invites confusion. One might be tempted to infer from the presence of the third condition that connectedness entails stronger properties than it actually does — in particular, that connected spaces are path-connected, which is false. The topologist's sine curve, for example, is connected and satisfies all three conditions, yet it is not path-connected: there is no continuous path from a point on the vertical segment to a point on the oscillatory curve. The definition, by including a consequence as though it were an independent requirement, obscures the distinction between connectedness and path-connectedness.

definition: A function f from the natural numbers to the natural numbers is computable if and only if (i) there exists a finite set of instructions that, when followed mechanically, produces f(n) for any input n, (ii) the set of instructions halts after finitely many steps for every input, and (iii) the instructions make no reference to infinite objects or completed infinities.
conditions: There exists a finite set of mechanical instructions producing f(n) for any n
The instructions halt after finitely many steps for every input
The instructions make no reference to infinite objects
counterexample: Consider Ackermann's function, which is computable in the standard sense — it is total and recursive. A finite set of instructions exists that halts after finitely many steps for every input. However, a natural formulation of Ackermann's function employs recursion on a pair of natural numbers, and at intermediate stages the recursion generates values of enormous size. One might argue that the intermediate computation implicitly references quantities so vast that they approach a kind of effective infinity. Yet the function is plainly computable. The third condition, if taken seriously, would raise doubts about functions that are universally accepted as computable.
missing_condition: The third condition conflates the intensional character of the instructions with the extensional nature of the computation; computability concerns whether the instructions produce the correct output in finitely many steps, not whether the instructions employ the concept of infinity in their formulation.
passage: One might define a computable function as one for which a finite set of mechanical instructions exists that halts in finitely many steps for every input and makes no reference to infinite objects. The first two conditions capture the essence of effective computability as understood since the work of Turing: a finite procedure that terminates on every input. The third might seem a natural supplement, reflecting the intuition that computation is an inherently finitary enterprise. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the third condition arises with functions whose natural description employs concepts that might be taken to reference infinity. Ackermann's function, for instance, is defined by a doubly recursive schema that generates intermediate values of stupendous magnitude — values that grow faster than any primitive recursive function. A strict reading of the prohibition on references to infinite objects might raise the objection that these intermediate values are, in a practical sense, beyond any finite computation, or that the recursive schema itself implicitly involves a kind of completed totality. Yet Ackermann's function is unambiguously computable: a Turing machine can be constructed that computes it, halting after finitely many steps for every input. The third condition, intended to preserve the finitary character of computation, in fact confuses the finiteness of the process with restrictions on the language in which the process is described. Computability is a property of what the instructions do, not of how they are phrased.

definition: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function from A to B that is injective, (ii) there exists a function from B to A that is injective, and (iii) there exists a function from A to B that is surjective.
conditions: There exists an injection from A to B
There exists an injection from B to A
There exists a surjection from A to B
counterexample: The three conditions, taken individually, do hold for sets of equal cardinality, but the definition is redundant in a potentially confusing way. By the Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder theorem, conditions (i) and (ii) alone suffice to establish that A and B have the same cardinality — a bijection from A to B must exist whenever there are injections in both directions. The third condition adds nothing. Moreover, condition (iii) alone is insufficient: there exists a surjection from the real numbers to the natural numbers (by choosing one natural number for each real), but the reals have strictly greater cardinality than the naturals.
missing_condition: The definition is not incorrect but is redundant: conditions (i) and (ii) together suffice by the Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder theorem, and the inclusion of (iii) as a separate condition may mislead the reader into thinking surjectivity provides an independent constraint.
passage: One might propose that two sets have the same cardinality if and only if there exists an injection from each to the other and a surjection from the first to the second. The first two conditions are together sufficient for equal cardinality, by the Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder theorem, which guarantees that mutual injectability entails the existence of a bijection. The third condition, requiring a surjection, adds nothing to the first two: if a bijection exists, it is automatically surjective. The definition is not false — its conditions are all satisfied when two sets have the same cardinality — but the inclusion of the third condition as though it were an independent requirement is misleading. A reader unfamiliar with the Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder theorem might suppose that the surjectivity condition contributes something essential, when in fact it is entirely redundant. Worse, one might infer from the definition that surjectivity alone provides evidence of equal cardinality, which is false: there exists a surjection from the real numbers to the natural numbers, yet the cardinality of the reals is strictly greater. The definition, by conflating a theorem with a definition, obscures the logical structure of the concept it purports to capture.

definition: A metric space (X, d) is complete if and only if (i) every Cauchy sequence in X converges to a point in X, (ii) X is closed as a subset of itself, and (iii) every bounded subset of X has a convergent subsequence.
conditions: Every Cauchy sequence in X converges to a point in X
X is closed as a subset of itself
Every bounded subset of X has a convergent subsequence
counterexample: Consider the real line with the usual metric. It is complete: every Cauchy sequence of real numbers converges to a real number. It is trivially closed as a subset of itself. But it is not the case that every bounded subset has a convergent subsequence in the sense that would make the space sequentially compact — rather, every bounded infinite subset has a convergent subsequence, which is the Bolzano-Weierstrass property. In fact, the third condition as stated characterises a much stronger property than completeness. The space of real-valued sequences with the supremum metric is complete but not sequentially compact: there exist bounded subsets with no convergent subsequence. All three conditions are thus not satisfied, even though the space is complete.
missing_condition: The third condition conflates completeness with sequential compactness; the former requires only that Cauchy sequences converge, while the latter requires that bounded sequences have convergent subsequences, and the two properties are distinct in infinite-dimensional spaces.
passage: One might define a complete metric space as one in which every Cauchy sequence converges, which is closed as a subset of itself, and in which every bounded subset has a convergent subsequence. The first condition is the standard definition of completeness, and the second is trivially satisfied by any topological space considered as a subset of itself. The third condition, however, goes well beyond completeness. It asserts a form of the Bolzano-Weierstrass property that characterises sequential compactness — a property far stronger than completeness. In finite-dimensional Euclidean space, a closed and bounded set is indeed compact, and the Bolzano-Weierstrass property holds. But in infinite-dimensional spaces the two properties diverge sharply. The space of bounded real-valued sequences, equipped with the supremum metric, is complete: every Cauchy sequence converges. But it is not sequentially compact: one can readily construct a bounded sequence of elements — for example, the sequence of standard basis vectors — that has no convergent subsequence. The third condition thus fails for a space that is indisputably complete. The definition, by including a condition drawn from the special features of finite-dimensional spaces, mistakes a consequence of completeness-plus-finite-dimensionality for a consequence of completeness alone.

definition: A set S of natural numbers is decidable if and only if (i) there exists a Turing machine that, given any natural number n as input, halts and outputs 'yes' if n is in S, (ii) the same machine halts and outputs 'no' if n is not in S, and (iii) the machine uses space bounded by a polynomial in the length of the input.
conditions: A Turing machine halts and outputs 'yes' for every n in S
The same machine halts and outputs 'no' for every n not in S
The machine uses space bounded by a polynomial in the input length
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers that encode valid proofs in first-order Peano arithmetic. This set is decidable: given a purported proof, one can mechanically verify each step and determine whether the proof is correct, halting with 'yes' or 'no' in every case. The first two conditions are met. However, the space required to verify a proof grows with the length of the proof, and for proofs of arbitrary length the verification may require space that exceeds any polynomial bound. The third condition may fail, yet the set is decidable in the standard sense.
missing_condition: The definition conflates decidability with membership in the complexity class PSPACE; decidability requires only that a Turing machine halt on every input with the correct answer, without any restriction on the resources consumed.
passage: One might define a decidable set of natural numbers as one for which a Turing machine exists that halts on every input, answering 'yes' for members and 'no' for non-members, while using space bounded by a polynomial in the input length. The first two conditions constitute the standard definition of decidability: the existence of a total procedure that correctly classifies every natural number. The third condition adds a resource constraint that is not part of the classical notion. Decidability, as formulated by Turing and subsequently codified in the theory of computation, places no restriction on the time or space a machine may use — it requires only that the machine eventually halt with the correct answer. The addition of a polynomial space bound transforms the definition from one of decidability into one of membership in the complexity class PSPACE. This is a far more restrictive requirement. Many decidable sets — for instance, those requiring exponential space for their recognition — would be excluded by the third condition. The set of encodings of valid proofs in first-order arithmetic is decidable, for one can check each step mechanically, but the space required grows without polynomial bound as the proofs lengthen. The definition, by conflating decidability with efficient decidability, excludes sets that every computability theorist would classify as decidable.

definition: A theory T in first-order logic is decidable if and only if (i) for every sentence S in the language of T, either S or its negation is provable in T, (ii) there exists an effective procedure to determine, for any sentence S, whether S is provable in T, and (iii) T is consistent.
conditions: For every sentence S, either S or its negation is provable in T
There exists an effective procedure to determine provability
T is consistent
counterexample: The first-order theory of the real numbers as an ordered field, as shown by Tarski, is decidable: there is an effective procedure that determines, for any sentence in its language, whether the sentence is a theorem. The theory is also consistent and complete — every sentence or its negation is provable. All three conditions are met. But now consider a decidable but incomplete theory, such as the theory of infinite sets in a pure equality language. This theory is decidable — an effective procedure exists to determine provability — and consistent, but it is not complete: some sentences are neither provable nor refutable. The first condition fails, yet the theory is decidable.
missing_condition: The definition incorrectly includes completeness as a necessary condition for decidability; a theory may be decidable without being complete, as decidability requires only that provability be effectively determinable, not that every sentence be settled.
passage: One might propose that a first-order theory is decidable if and only if it is complete (every sentence or its negation is provable), there exists an effective procedure to determine provability, and the theory is consistent. This definition captures several prominent examples: the theory of dense linear orders without endpoints, for instance, is complete, consistent, and decidable. Tarski's theory of real-closed fields is likewise complete, consistent, and decidable. The three conditions appear to be satisfied by all the standard examples. Yet completeness is not a necessary condition for decidability. A theory is decidable if there is an effective procedure that correctly determines, for every sentence, whether it is a theorem — and this is possible even when some sentences are neither provable nor refutable. One need only consider a decidable but incomplete theory. The theory of equality with infinitely many objects is decidable — one can effectively determine whether a sentence is provable — and consistent, but it is not complete: the sentence asserting the existence of exactly seventeen objects is neither provable nor refutable.
