The first condition fails, yet the theory is decidable by any standard characterisation. The definition mistakes a feature of the most celebrated decidable theories for a requirement of decidability itself.

definition: A sequence of functions f_1, f_2, f_3, ... converges uniformly to a function f on a set D if and only if (i) for every point x in D, f_n(x) converges to f(x), (ii) for every epsilon > 0 there exists an N such that for all n > N and all x in D, |f_n(x) - f(x)| < epsilon, and (iii) the limit function f is continuous on D.
conditions: For every x in D, f_n(x) converges to f(x)
For every epsilon > 0 there exists N such that for all n > N and all x in D, |f_n(x) - f(x)| < epsilon
The limit function f is continuous on D
counterexample: Consider the sequence of functions f_n(x) = x^n on the interval [0, 1]. The pointwise limit is f(x) = 0 for x in [0, 1) and f(1) = 1. This sequence does not converge uniformly, so it does not serve as a case against the definition in the intended direction. Instead consider f_n(x) = (1/n)floor(nx) on [0,1], which converges uniformly to the identity f(x) = x — here the limit is continuous. For a genuine case revealing the insufficiency of the third condition, note that the sequence f_n(x) = x/n converges uniformly to f(x) = 0 on any bounded set, and f is continuous. But now consider f_n = g + 1/n where g is a fixed bounded discontinuous function. The convergence is uniform, but the limit g is discontinuous. The third condition fails, yet the convergence is uniform.
missing_condition: The third condition conflates a consequence of uniform convergence of continuous functions with a defining property; uniform convergence does not require the limit to be continuous — it requires only the uniform epsilon-N condition — and the limit may be discontinuous when the approximating functions are themselves discontinuous.
passage: One might define uniform convergence of a sequence of functions on a set D as requiring pointwise convergence, the existence of a uniform bound on the error for all points and all sufficiently large n, and continuity of the limit function. The first two conditions together constitute the standard definition of uniform convergence: condition (ii) is the essential uniform epsilon-N requirement, and condition (i) is entailed by it. The third condition, however, is not part of the definition at all. It is a theorem that the uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous, but this theorem has a hypothesis — the continuity of the approximating functions — that the definition omits. If the functions f_n are not themselves continuous, their uniform limit need not be either. Consider the sequence f_n = g + 1/n, where g is a fixed bounded function with a discontinuity. The sequence converges uniformly to g, since the error |f_n(x) - g(x)| = 1/n for all x, which can be made less than any epsilon by choosing n large enough. The convergence is uniform by condition (ii), and pointwise by condition (i). But the limit g is discontinuous, so condition (iii) fails. The definition, by including continuity of the limit as a defining condition, confuses a consequence that holds under special hypotheses with a requirement of the concept itself.

definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point c if and only if (i) f is defined at c, (ii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists, and (iii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c equals f(c).
conditions: f is defined at c
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists
The limit equals f(c)
counterexample: Consider the function f defined on the real line by f(x) = sin(1/x) for x not equal to zero, and f(0) = 0. At every point c other than zero, the function is continuous by the standard epsilon-delta criterion, and the three conditions are satisfied. At c = 0, however, the limit of sin(1/x) as x approaches 0 does not exist, because the function oscillates between -1 and 1 with increasing rapidity. The definition correctly identifies the discontinuity at zero. But now consider the related function g(x) = x sin(1/x) for x not equal to zero, and g(0) = 0. Here the limit as x approaches 0 does exist and equals 0 = g(0), so all three conditions are satisfied, and the definition declares g continuous at 0. Yet g is not differentiable at 0, and its behaviour near 0 — oscillating infinitely often — is so wild that in many analytical contexts it behaves quite unlike a function that is continuous in the intuitively robust sense.
missing_condition: The definition captures the topological notion of continuity but does not distinguish between functions that are continuous in a tame, analytically well-behaved manner and those whose continuity is of a pathological character, suggesting that continuity as defined may be too permissive a notion for contexts requiring smooth or well-behaved functions.
passage: One might define continuity of a real-valued function at a point in the familiar manner: the function is defined at the point, its limit exists there, and the limit equals the function's value. This definition, traceable to Cauchy and refined by Weierstrass, has served analysis admirably for more than a century. It captures the intuitive idea that a continuous function has no gaps or jumps. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition — or at least its permissiveness — may be found among the pathological functions that nineteenth-century analysis brought to light. The function g(x) = x sin(1/x) for x not zero, with g(0) = 0, satisfies all three conditions at the origin: it is defined there, the limit exists (since |g(x)| is bounded by |x|, which tends to 0), and the limit equals the value. The definition declares g continuous at 0. Yet near the origin, g oscillates infinitely often, and its graph cannot be drawn by any draughtsman — it changes direction more and more rapidly as one approaches zero. The function is not differentiable at 0, and its behaviour is, in an intuitive sense, thoroughly wild. One might observe that the epsilon-delta definition of continuity, for all its precision, is more permissive than the intuitive concept it was designed to formalise. Functions that satisfy the definition may exhibit behaviour that is, in analytical practice, regarded as pathological. The definition tells us that the function has no gap at the point, but it does not tell us that the function is well-behaved there in any stronger sense.

definition: An infinite series S = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + ... converges if and only if (i) the sequence of partial sums S_n = a_1 + ... + a_n has a finite limit L, (ii) for every positive epsilon there exists N such that |S_n - L| < epsilon for all n > N, and (iii) the terms a_n tend to zero as n tends to infinity.
conditions: The sequence of partial sums has a finite limit L
For every epsilon > 0 there exists N such that |S_n - L| < epsilon for all n > N
The terms a_n tend to zero
counterexample: Consider the harmonic series 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 + ... . The terms 1/n tend to zero as n tends to infinity, so condition (iii) is satisfied. Yet the sequence of partial sums grows without bound — it can be shown that the partial sums exceed any given number if one takes sufficiently many terms. Conditions (i) and (ii) fail. The definition correctly identifies the harmonic series as divergent. But the difficulty runs in the other direction as well: condition (iii) is presented as part of a necessary-and-sufficient characterisation, when in fact conditions (i) and (ii) alone are both necessary and sufficient for convergence. Condition (iii) is a consequence of (i) and (ii) and adds nothing to the definition, yet its inclusion suggests, misleadingly, that it is an independent requirement.
passage: One might define the convergence of an infinite series by requiring that the partial sums approach a finite limit, that this approach satisfy the usual epsilon criterion, and that the individual terms tend to zero. This definition appears to provide three independent conditions whose joint satisfaction guarantees convergence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation — or rather, its redundancy — is the harmonic series. The terms 1/n do indeed tend to zero, satisfying condition (iii), yet the series diverges: the partial sums grow without bound. This shows that condition (iii) is not sufficient for convergence. But more importantly, conditions (i) and (ii) are together both necessary and sufficient — they are, in fact, simply two ways of stating the same requirement, the second being the epsilon-delta formulation of the first. Condition (iii) is a logical consequence of (i) and (ii): if the partial sums converge, then the terms must tend to zero, for a_n = S_n - S_{n-1}, and both S_n and S_{n-1} tend to L. The definition thus includes a condition that is not independent of the other two, and its inclusion creates the misleading impression that the vanishing of the terms is an additional requirement rather than an automatic consequence. One might observe that a well-constructed definition should contain only independent conditions, and that the inclusion of a redundant condition, however true, invites confusion about what is doing the logical work.

definition: A sequence of propositions constitutes a valid mathematical proof of a theorem T if and only if (i) each proposition in the sequence is either an axiom, a previously established theorem, or a logical consequence of earlier propositions in the sequence, (ii) the final proposition in the sequence is T, and (iii) every step of logical inference conforms to a recognised rule of deduction.
conditions: Each proposition is an axiom, established theorem, or logical consequence of prior steps
The final proposition is T
Every inference conforms to a recognised rule of deduction
counterexample: Consider the following proof that there exist irrational numbers a and b such that a^b is rational. Let a = sqrt(2) and b = sqrt(2). Either sqrt(2)^{sqrt(2)} is rational or it is irrational. If it is rational, then we have found our example with a = b = sqrt(2). If it is irrational, then let a = sqrt(2)^{sqrt(2)} and b = sqrt(2); then a^b = (sqrt(2)^{sqrt(2)})^{sqrt(2)} = sqrt(2)^2 = 2, which is rational. In either case, the theorem is proved. Every step conforms to the rules of logic, every proposition follows from its predecessors, and the conclusion is established. All three conditions are met. Yet many mathematicians would regard this proof as unsatisfying, because it does not tell us which case actually holds — it establishes the existence of the desired numbers without identifying them.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that a proof be constructive or informative — that it identify the specific objects whose existence it establishes — and thereby admits proofs that, while logically valid, fail to provide the kind of mathematical understanding that working mathematicians typically seek.
passage: One might define a valid proof as a sequence of propositions in which each step follows from axioms, established theorems, or the logical consequences of preceding steps, terminating in the theorem to be proved, with every inference conforming to a recognised rule of deduction. This definition is a faithful rendering of the notion of formal proof as it has developed since Frege and Hilbert. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition — or at least its distance from mathematical practice — is the classical proof of the existence of irrational numbers a and b such that a^b is rational. The proof proceeds by considering sqrt(2)^{sqrt(2)}: either it is rational or it is not. If rational, set a = b = sqrt(2). If irrational, set a = sqrt(2)^{sqrt(2)} and b = sqrt(2); then a^b = 2. In either case, irrational numbers with the desired property exist. The proof is impeccable by the standards of the definition: every step is a logical consequence of what precedes it, the conclusion follows, and every inference is valid. All three conditions are met. Yet a considerable number of mathematicians, particularly those of a constructivist persuasion, would regard this proof as deficient, for it does not tell us which pair of numbers actually satisfies the theorem. It establishes that a certain state of affairs obtains without identifying the objects that constitute it. One might observe that the definition captures the logical validity of proof but not the informational content, and that a definition which counts as equally valid a proof that identifies its objects and a proof that merely asserts their existence fails to distinguish between two fundamentally different kinds of mathematical demonstration.

definition: A relation R on a set S is an equivalence relation if and only if (i) R is reflexive — every element of S bears R to itself, (ii) R is symmetric — if a bears R to b then b bears R to a, and (iii) R is transitive — if a bears R to b and b bears R to c, then a bears R to c.
conditions: R is reflexive: every element bears R to itself
R is symmetric: if aRb then bRa
R is transitive: if aRb and bRc then aRc
counterexample: Consider the relation 'is within one unit of' on the real numbers — that is, the relation that holds between x and y whenever |x - y| <= 1. This relation is reflexive, for |x - x| = 0 <= 1 for every x. It is symmetric, for if |x - y| <= 1 then |y - x| <= 1. But it is not transitive: 0 is within one unit of 0.8, and 0.8 is within one unit of 1.6, but 0 is not within one unit of 1.6. The definition correctly excludes this relation. Yet the case is instructive because the relation satisfies two of the three conditions and is, in everyday reasoning, often treated as though it were an equivalence — people routinely classify objects as 'approximately equal' and reason as though approximate equality were transitive, which it is not.
passage: One might define an equivalence relation by requiring reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity. This definition is standard and has proved indispensable in mathematics. Yet its application to informal reasoning reveals a difficulty that is worth examining. Consider the relation 'is within one unit of' on the real numbers, defined by the condition that |x - y| <= 1. This relation is reflexive: every number is within one unit of itself. It is symmetric: if x is within one unit of y, then y is within one unit of x. These two conditions are satisfied without difficulty. But the relation is not transitive. The number 0 is within one unit of 0.8, and 0.8 is within one unit of 1.6, but 0 is not within one unit of 1.6. The definition correctly excludes this relation from the class of equivalence relations. Yet the case is instructive because relations of approximate equality — 'is roughly the same size as,' 'is approximately the same colour as,' 'is nearly as heavy as' — are ubiquitous in ordinary reasoning, and they are routinely treated as though they were equivalences. People classify objects into groups on the basis of approximate similarity and reason as though membership in such groups were transitive. The failure of transitivity in approximate relations is a persistent source of error in informal thought. One might observe that the mathematical definition of equivalence is more demanding than the informal concept it is often taken to represent, and that the gap between the two is a source of systematic confusion in the application of mathematical concepts to empirical reasoning.

definition: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than 1, (ii) n has no positive divisors other than 1 and n itself, and (iii) n is odd.
conditions: n is greater than 1
n has no positive divisors other than 1 and n
n is odd
counterexample: The number 2 is greater than 1 and has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. It satisfies conditions (i) and (ii). Yet 2 is even, not odd, so condition (iii) excludes it from the class of prime numbers. This is plainly incorrect: 2 is universally recognised as the smallest prime, and its primality is essential to the fundamental theorem of arithmetic. The definition, by including the condition that a prime must be odd, excludes the only even prime.
passage: One might define a prime number as a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself and is odd. This definition appears to capture the common observation that, with a single exception, all prime numbers are indeed odd. The vast majority of primes — 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, and so on without end — are odd numbers, and it might seem natural to include oddness as a defining property. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by the number 2. Two is greater than 1. It has no positive divisors other than 1 and 2. It satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) with complete straightforwardness. But it is not odd — it is the smallest even number — and condition (iii) therefore excludes it from the definition. This exclusion is evidently wrong. The number 2 is universally recognised as prime, and its primality plays an essential role in number theory: the fundamental theorem of arithmetic, which states that every natural number greater than 1 has a unique prime factorisation, depends upon 2 being counted among the primes. One might observe that the definition has confused a property that is true of almost all primes with a property that is necessary for primality. The oddness of primes greater than 2 is a consequence of the definition of primality, not a part of it, and the inclusion of this consequence as a defining condition produces a definition that is too restrictive by exactly one case — but that one case is of considerable importance.

definition: A rule f from a set A to a set B is a function if and only if (i) f assigns to every element of A an element of B, (ii) f assigns to each element of A at most one element of B, and (iii) the rule f can be expressed by an explicit algebraic formula.
conditions: f assigns to every element of A an element of B
f assigns at most one element to each element of A
f can be expressed by an explicit algebraic formula
counterexample: Consider the Dirichlet function, defined on the real numbers, which assigns 1 to every rational number and 0 to every irrational number. This rule assigns to every real number exactly one value, satisfying conditions (i) and (ii). But it cannot be expressed by any algebraic formula — it is defined by a case distinction based on the number-theoretic property of rationality. Condition (iii) excludes it. Yet the Dirichlet function is a perfectly legitimate function in the modern sense and plays an important role in analysis as a standard example of a function that is nowhere continuous.
missing_condition: The definition includes the requirement that a function be expressible by an algebraic formula, which is a relic of an older conception of function; modern mathematics recognises as functions any well-defined assignments from domain to codomain, regardless of whether they admit of algebraic expression.
passage: One might define a function as a rule that assigns to every element of a given set exactly one element of another set and that can be expressed by an explicit algebraic formula. This definition has respectable historical antecedents: in the eighteenth century, Euler and his contemporaries understood functions primarily as algebraic expressions — polynomials, trigonometric expressions, exponentials, and their compositions. Conditions (i) and (ii) ensure that the assignment is total and unambiguous, and condition (iii) ensures that the rule has a definite, explicit character. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the Dirichlet function, which assigns 1 to every rational number and 0 to every irrational number. This rule satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) without difficulty: every real number receives exactly one value. But it cannot be written as an algebraic formula. It is defined by a case distinction — rational or irrational — that has no algebraic expression. The definition therefore excludes it. Yet the Dirichlet function is a perfectly legitimate function in the modern mathematical sense. It appears in every introductory analysis course as the standard example of a function that is nowhere Riemann integrable and nowhere continuous. One might observe that condition (iii) reflects a historical prejudice rather than a mathematical necessity. The development of analysis in the nineteenth century, from Dirichlet through Riemann to Lebesgue, progressively liberated the concept of function from the requirement of algebraic expressibility, and a definition that retains this requirement is too narrow to accommodate the functions that modern analysis actually employs.

definition: A collection C is a set if and only if (i) C is composed of well-defined, distinct objects, (ii) for every object x, it is determinately either the case that x belongs to C or that x does not, and (iii) C is itself an object of the same kind as its members and may therefore be a member of other collections.
conditions: C is composed of well-defined, distinct objects
For every object x, membership in C is determinate
C is itself an object that may be a member of other collections
counterexample: Consider the collection R of all sets that do not contain themselves as members. Each member of R is a well-defined set, membership is determinate (a set either contains itself or it does not), and R is taken to be a set that may itself belong to other collections. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet as Russell demonstrated in 1901, the question of whether R belongs to itself generates a contradiction: if R is a member of R, then by definition it is not; if it is not, then by definition it is. The collection R cannot coherently be regarded as a set.
missing_condition: The definition permits unrestricted set formation — any well-defined collection counts as a set — and thereby admits collections that generate paradox, suggesting that the concept of set must be constrained by additional principles (such as those of Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory) that restrict which collections qualify as sets.
passage: One might define a set as a collection of well-defined, distinct objects for which membership is determinate and which is itself an object capable of belonging to further collections. This naive definition has an appealing simplicity: it permits any well-defined collection to count as a set, places no restriction on what objects may be collected, and treats sets and their members as objects of the same logical type. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition was discovered by Russell at the very beginning of the twentieth century. Consider the collection R of all sets that do not contain themselves as members. Each member of R is a well-defined set, and for any given set it is determinately the case that it either contains itself or does not. R appears to satisfy all three conditions. But ask whether R is a member of itself. If it is, then it is a set that contains itself, and by the definition of R it does not belong to R — a contradiction. If it is not a member of itself, then it is a set that does not contain itself, and by the definition of R it does belong to R — again a contradiction. The collection R, despite satisfying all three conditions, cannot coherently exist as a set. One might observe that the difficulty arises from the definition's permissiveness: by allowing any well-defined collection to count as a set, it admits collections whose very definition is self-undermining. The remedy, as Zermelo and others showed, is to impose restrictions on which collections may be formed, but the naive definition contains no such restrictions and is therefore vulnerable to paradox.

definition: A geometric figure F possesses a symmetry if and only if (i) there exists a rigid transformation T of the plane (a rotation, reflection, or translation) that maps F onto itself, (ii) T preserves the distances between all pairs of points in F, and (iii) T is non-trivial — that is, T is not the identity transformation.
conditions: There exists a rigid transformation T that maps F onto itself
T preserves all pairwise distances in F
T is not the identity transformation
counterexample: Consider a circle centred at the origin. A rotation of the plane through any angle about the origin maps the circle onto itself and preserves all distances. The circle possesses rotational symmetry through every angle, and since the only identity rotation is through zero degrees, every non-zero rotation is non-trivial. All three conditions are satisfied for uncountably many transformations. Yet this abundance of symmetries means that the definition assigns to the circle the same kind of symmetry — rigid-motion symmetry — as it assigns to a square, which has only eight rigid-motion symmetries. The definition cannot distinguish between these qualitatively different situations because it classifies symmetry as a binary property (the figure either has a non-trivial rigid symmetry or it does not) rather than as a structured quantity.
missing_condition: The definition captures whether a figure possesses at least one non-trivial symmetry but fails to characterise the structure of the symmetry group — its order, its algebraic type, whether it is finite or infinite — and thereby treats the vastly different symmetry structures of the circle and the square as instances of the same property.
passage: One might define a symmetry of a geometric figure as a non-trivial rigid transformation that maps the figure onto itself while preserving all distances. This definition captures the fundamental idea that symmetry involves invariance under transformation: a figure is symmetric if it looks the same after being rotated, reflected, or translated. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when we consider how it classifies figures with very different symmetry structures. A square centred at the origin admits exactly eight non-trivial rigid symmetries: four rotations (through 90, 180, 270, and 360 degrees, the last being the identity, so three non-trivial rotations) and four reflections. A circle centred at the origin, by contrast, admits uncountably many: a rotation through any angle maps the circle onto itself, and a reflection across any diameter does likewise. The definition, applied to each figure, simply pronounces that each possesses a non-trivial symmetry — which is true in both cases. But this verdict fails to capture the profound qualitative difference between the two situations. The symmetries of the square form a finite group of order eight — the dihedral group — while the symmetries of the circle form the infinite orthogonal group O(2). One might observe that a satisfactory account of symmetry must attend not only to the existence of symmetry-preserving transformations but to the algebraic structure of the collection of all such transformations, for it is this structure, rather than the mere existence of individual symmetries, that characterises the symmetry of a figure.

definition: A set S is infinite if and only if (i) S is not empty, (ii) for every natural number n, S contains at least n distinct elements, and (iii) S cannot be enumerated — that is, the elements of S cannot be placed in a one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers.
conditions: S is not empty
For every natural number n, S contains at least n distinct elements
S cannot be placed in one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers itself: {0, 1, 2, 3, ...}. This set is not empty. For every natural number n, it contains at least n elements. It satisfies conditions (i) and (ii). But the set of natural numbers can obviously be placed in one-to-one correspondence with itself — the identity mapping serves — so condition (iii) is not satisfied. The definition therefore excludes the natural numbers from the class of infinite sets. Yet the natural numbers are the paradigmatic example of an infinite set. The definition has confused 'infinite' with 'uncountably infinite,' excluding all countably infinite sets.
passage: One might define an infinite set as a non-empty set that contains at least n elements for every natural number n and that cannot be enumerated by the natural numbers. This definition attempts to capture two aspects of infinity: unboundedness (the set is larger than any finite collection) and unenumerability (the set's elements cannot be listed). Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the set of natural numbers itself. The natural numbers form a non-empty collection, and for every natural number n, the set contains at least n elements — indeed, it contains infinitely many more. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But the natural numbers can be placed in one-to-one correspondence with themselves by the identity mapping, and condition (iii) therefore fails. The definition excludes the natural numbers from the class of infinite sets. This is an extraordinary result, for the natural numbers are the very paradigm of an infinite set — indeed, they are the set whose infinity was first recognised and whose properties motivated the entire theory of the infinite. One might observe that the definition has conflated two distinct notions: infinity (having more elements than any finite set) and uncountability (having more elements than the natural numbers). Cantor's great discovery was precisely that these are different — that there are infinite sets of different sizes — but the definition, by combining them, produces a concept that excludes countably infinite sets, which are the most fundamental examples of infinity in mathematics.

definition: Two algebraic structures A and B are isomorphic if and only if (i) there exists a bijective mapping f from the elements of A to the elements of B, (ii) f preserves the algebraic operations — that is, f(a * b) = f(a) * f(b) for all elements a, b in A, and (iii) A and B have the same number of elements.
conditions: There exists a bijective mapping f from A to B
f preserves the algebraic operations
A and B have the same number of elements
counterexample: Consider any two finite groups of the same order — say, the cyclic group Z_4 (the integers modulo 4 under addition) and the Klein four-group V_4 (comprising the identity and three elements each of order 2, under componentwise addition modulo 2). Both groups have four elements, so condition (iii) is satisfied. But no bijection between them can preserve the group operation, because Z_4 contains an element of order 4 while every non-identity element of V_4 has order 2. Conditions (i) and (ii) cannot simultaneously be satisfied. The definition's third condition — equal cardinality — is redundant, being a consequence of the existence of a bijection, and its presence creates the misleading impression that equal cardinality is a substantive condition when it is in fact automatically guaranteed by condition (i).
passage: One might define isomorphism between two algebraic structures as the existence of a bijective, operation-preserving mapping between them, supplemented by the condition that the two structures have the same number of elements. This definition appears to give three independent conditions: bijectivity, preservation of operations, and equal cardinality. Yet a moment's reflection reveals that condition (iii) is entirely redundant. Any bijective mapping between two sets establishes, by its very existence, that the sets have the same cardinality. If a bijection from A to B exists, then A and B must have the same number of elements; if they have different numbers of elements, no bijection can exist. Condition (iii) is a logical consequence of condition (i) and contributes nothing to the definition. A more instructive case concerns the distinction the definition fails to capture. The cyclic group Z_4 and the Klein four-group V_4 both have four elements. Condition (iii) is satisfied. Bijections between them certainly exist — there are 24 such bijections, in fact. But none of these bijections preserves the group operation, because Z_4 has an element of order 4, while every non-identity element of V_4 has order 2. The two groups are not isomorphic despite having the same cardinality. One might observe that the heart of isomorphism lies entirely in conditions (i) and (ii) — the existence of a bijection that respects the algebraic structure — and that the addition of condition (iii) is not only redundant but actively misleading, for it suggests that matching cardinality is a substantive requirement rather than an automatic consequence of what is already demanded.

definition: A relation R on a set S is an equivalence relation if and only if (i) R is reflexive — for every element x in S, xRx holds, (ii) R is symmetric — for all x, y in S, if xRy then yRx, and (iii) R is transitive — for all x, y, z in S, if xRy and yRz then xRz.
conditions: R is reflexive
R is symmetric
R is transitive
counterexample: Consider the relation of approximate equality on the real numbers, defined by saying that x is approximately equal to y if and only if |x - y| < 1. This relation is reflexive, since |x - x| = 0 < 1 for every x. It is symmetric, since |x - y| = |y - x|. But it is not transitive: 0 is approximately equal to 0.9, and 0.9 is approximately equal to 1.8, but 0 is not approximately equal to 1.8, since |0 - 1.8| = 1.8, which is not less than 1. The definition correctly identifies this as failing to be an equivalence relation. Yet the definition also admits as equivalence relations certain relations that partition a set into classes that violate the intuitive purpose of equivalence — for example, the trivial relation in which every element is related to every other element satisfies all three conditions but fails to make any distinctions at all.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the equivalence classes induced by the relation be non-trivial — that they partition the set into more than one class — and thereby admits the universal relation as an equivalence relation, which, while formally correct, undermines the classificatory purpose that equivalence relations are typically intended to serve.
passage: One might define an equivalence relation on a set as a relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. This definition is a cornerstone of modern mathematics, and its power lies in the theorem that every equivalence relation partitions its underlying set into disjoint equivalence classes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency — or at least the excessive permissiveness — of this definition arises when one considers the trivial case. The universal relation on a set S, defined by saying that xRy holds for all x and y in S, is reflexive (every element is related to itself), symmetric (if xRy then yRx, since both hold unconditionally), and transitive (if xRy and yRz then xRz, again unconditionally). All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition pronounces the universal relation an equivalence relation. Yet this relation partitions S into a single equivalence class containing every element, and thus makes no distinctions whatsoever. The purpose of introducing equivalence relations is typically to classify elements into groups that share some common property while differing from elements in other groups. A relation that places everything in one class defeats this purpose. One might observe that the definition, while formally impeccable, is more generous than the mathematical practice that motivates it. In practice, one seeks equivalence relations that yield an informative partition — one with more than one class — and the definition's silence on this point allows it to admit relations that, though technically valid, are mathematically vacuous.

definition: A subset K of a metric space is compact if and only if (i) K is closed, (ii) K is bounded, and (iii) every sequence in K has a subsequence that converges to a point in K.
conditions: K is closed
K is bounded
Every sequence in K has a convergent subsequence with limit in K
counterexample: Consider the closed unit ball in the space of continuous functions on [0,1] equipped with the supremum metric — that is, the set of all continuous functions f with ||f|| at most 1. This set is closed and bounded. Yet by a classical result, not every sequence in this set has a convergent subsequence: one may construct a sequence of continuous functions, each of norm 1, no subsequence of which converges uniformly. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) fails. The definition correctly identifies this set as non-compact. However, the difficulty is that conditions (i) and (ii) are presented as though they contribute independently to the characterisation, when in fact, in a general metric space, the conjunction of (i) and (ii) does not imply (iii). The equivalence of closed-and-bounded with sequential compactness is a theorem about Euclidean space (the Heine-Borel theorem), not a general truth.
passage: One might characterise compactness of a subset of a metric space by requiring that the set be closed, bounded, and such that every sequence has a convergent subsequence. In Euclidean spaces, the celebrated theorem of Heine and Borel establishes that closedness and boundedness together entail the sequential compactness condition, so that the three requirements are not independent: (iii) follows from (i) and (ii). This has led to a widespread but misleading impression that closed-and-bounded is synonymous with compact. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this impression arises in infinite-dimensional spaces. Consider the closed unit ball in the space C[0,1] of continuous functions on the unit interval, equipped with the supremum norm. This set is closed — the limit of any convergent sequence of functions with norm at most 1 itself has norm at most 1. It is bounded — every function in the set has norm at most 1. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But condition (iii) fails: one can construct a sequence of continuous functions in the unit ball no subsequence of which converges uniformly. The set is closed and bounded but not compact. One might observe that the definition's three conditions, far from being mutually reinforcing, stand in different logical relationships depending on the ambient space. In finite-dimensional spaces, (iii) is a consequence of (i) and (ii); in infinite-dimensional spaces, it is not. The definition does not mark this distinction, and its inclusion of all three conditions as though they were independently needed obscures the structural fact that compactness is a stronger property than closed-and-boundedness once one leaves the comforting realm of Euclidean geometry.

definition: A function f from a topological space X to a topological space Y is continuous if and only if (i) f is defined at every point of X, (ii) for every open set U in Y, the preimage f^{-1}(U) is open in X, and (iii) f preserves the limit of every convergent sequence — that is, if x_n converges to x in X, then f(x_n) converges to f(x) in Y.
conditions: f is defined at every point of X
The preimage of every open set in Y is open in X
f preserves limits of convergent sequences
counterexample: Consider a function from an uncountable product of copies of the real line (with the product topology) to the real numbers that is continuous in the sense of condition (ii) — every preimage of an open set is open. Such a function automatically preserves limits of convergent sequences. But now consider the converse direction: in a general topological space that is not first-countable, condition (iii) does not imply condition (ii). One can construct functions on uncountable product spaces that preserve limits of all convergent sequences yet fail to have open preimages, because the topology of such spaces is not determined by their convergent sequences alone.
passage: One might define continuity of a function between topological spaces by requiring that the function be everywhere defined, that preimages of open sets be open, and that limits of convergent sequences be preserved. In metric spaces and other first-countable spaces, conditions (ii) and (iii) are equivalent, and either may be taken as the definition. This equivalence has encouraged the view that sequential preservation of limits and the open-preimage condition are merely two ways of stating the same requirement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this view arises in spaces that are not first-countable. Consider an uncountable product of copies of the real line with the product topology. In such a space, the topology is not determined by convergent sequences: there exist subsets that are sequentially closed (closed under limits of sequences) but not closed in the topology. Correspondingly, there exist functions defined on such spaces that preserve limits of every convergent sequence — condition (iii) is satisfied — yet whose preimages of certain open sets are not open — condition (ii) fails. The function is sequentially continuous but not topologically continuous. One might observe that the definition, by listing both conditions as though they were equivalent and mutually reinforcing, conceals a genuine logical gap that opens up beyond the familiar setting of metric spaces. In general topology, sequential continuity is strictly weaker than continuity, and a definition that presents the two as jointly necessary obscures this important distinction.

definition: A sequence of functions f_1, f_2, f_3, ... converges to a function f on a domain D if and only if (i) for every point x in D, the sequence f_n(x) converges to f(x), (ii) the convergence at each point is monotone — for every x, the sequence |f_n(x) - f(x)| is eventually decreasing, and (iii) the limit function f is of the same character as the approximating functions — if each f_n is continuous, then f is continuous.
conditions: For every point x in D, f_n(x) converges to f(x)
The convergence is monotone at each point
If each f_n is continuous, the limit f is continuous
counterexample: Consider the sequence of functions f_n on [0,1] defined by f_n(x) = x^n. For each x in [0,1), this sequence converges to 0; at x = 1 it converges to 1. The convergence at each point in [0,1) is indeed monotone, since x^n decreases as n increases when 0 < x < 1. Each f_n is continuous. Yet the limit function — which equals 0 on [0,1) and 1 at x = 1 — is not continuous. Condition (iii) fails. The definition's inclusion of condition (iii) as a requirement reveals not a deficiency of the sequence but a deficiency of the definition: it demands that pointwise convergence preserve continuity, when in fact the preservation of continuity requires a stronger mode of convergence — uniform convergence — that the definition does not mention.
passage: One might propose that a sequence of functions converges to a limit function when the convergence is pointwise, monotone, and preserves the character of the approximating functions — in particular, when continuous functions converge to a continuous limit. This definition attempts to combine the basic notion of pointwise convergence with regularity conditions that would ensure the limit function is well-behaved. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the classical sequence f_n(x) = x^n on the unit interval [0,1]. Each function in the sequence is continuous, and for each x in [0,1) the values x^n converge to 0 monotonically, since raising a number less than 1 to higher powers yields smaller results. At x = 1, the sequence is constant at 1. The pointwise limit is the function that equals 0 on [0,1) and 1 at x = 1. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) is violated: the limit function is discontinuous despite the continuity of each approximant. One might observe that the definition, by including condition (iii) as a requirement, demands something that pointwise convergence cannot guarantee. The preservation of continuity under limits requires not merely that the functions converge at each point but that they converge at a rate that does not vary too wildly from point to point — a condition captured by the notion of uniform convergence. The definition's three conditions are therefore not mutually satisfiable in all cases of pointwise convergence, and the definition conflates two distinct modes of convergence that ought to be kept separate.

definition: A mathematical argument A constitutes a proof by contradiction of a proposition P if and only if (i) A assumes the negation of P, (ii) A derives from that assumption, together with accepted axioms and previously established results, a logical contradiction, and (iii) A concludes that P must therefore be true.
conditions: A assumes the negation of P
A derives a contradiction from the assumption
A concludes that P is true
counterexample: Consider the classical proof that the square root of 2 is irrational. One assumes that the square root of 2 is rational — say, equal to p/q in lowest terms — and derives a contradiction by showing that both p and q must be even, contrary to the assumption that p/q is in lowest terms. The proof satisfies all three conditions. But now consider a purported proof by contradiction in which one assumes the negation of P and derives, after many steps, a proposition that contradicts not an axiom or established theorem but the negation of P itself. This is not a genuine contradiction but merely a demonstration that the negation of P implies P — which, by classical logic, establishes P without any contradiction having been reached in the usual sense.
missing_condition: The definition does not specify that the contradiction must be between two independently established propositions rather than between the assumption and its own negation, and thereby admits as proofs by contradiction arguments that are, in logical structure, direct proofs disguised as indirect ones.
passage: One might define a proof by contradiction as an argument that assumes the negation of the proposition to be proved, derives a logical contradiction, and concludes the truth of the proposition. This definition captures the standard structure of reductio ad absurdum as taught in every course on mathematical reasoning. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation — or at least its failure to discriminate among structurally different kinds of argument — arises when one considers what counts as a contradiction. In the classical proof that the square root of 2 is irrational, one assumes rationality, derives that the numerator and denominator are both even (contradicting the assumption that the fraction is in lowest terms), and concludes irrationality. The contradiction is genuine: two independently derived propositions — that p/q is in lowest terms and that both p and q are even — are mutually inconsistent. All three conditions are satisfied in the clearest possible way. But now suppose one assumes the negation of P and, through a lengthy chain of reasoning, derives P itself. One has shown that the negation of P implies P, which in classical logic entails P. The definition counts this as a proof by contradiction: the negation was assumed, a contradiction was derived (between the assumed negation of P and the derived P), and P was concluded. Yet the argument is, in its logical structure, a direct proof: one has established P from the axioms and established results alone, and the assumption of the negation played no essential role. One might observe that the definition's silence on the character of the contradiction permits it to classify essentially direct proofs as indirect, obscuring a distinction of logical structure that mathematicians rightly regard as significant.

definition: A natural number n greater than 1 is prime if and only if (i) n has no positive divisors other than 1 and n itself, (ii) n cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers both greater than 1, and (iii) n is odd or n equals 2.
conditions: n has no divisors other than 1 and itself
n cannot be expressed as a product of two numbers greater than 1
n is odd or n equals 2
counterexample: Consider the number 2. It has no positive divisors other than 1 and 2; it cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers both greater than 1; and it satisfies condition (iii) since it equals 2. All three conditions are met, and 2 is correctly identified as prime. Now consider conditions (i) and (ii) alone. They are logically equivalent: a number greater than 1 has no divisors other than 1 and itself if and only if it cannot be expressed as a product of two numbers both greater than 1. Condition (ii) adds nothing to (i). Meanwhile, condition (iii) — that the number be odd or equal to 2 — is a consequence of (i), since the only even number with no divisors other than 1 and itself is 2. All three conditions are redundant upon (i) alone.
passage: One might characterise a prime number as a natural number greater than 1 that has no divisors other than 1 and itself, cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers both greater than 1, and is either odd or equal to 2. This definition attempts to provide three independent conditions whose satisfaction guarantees primality. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation — or rather, its redundancy — is furnished by a careful examination of the logical relations among the conditions. Conditions (i) and (ii) are logically equivalent: to say that n has no divisors other than 1 and n is precisely to say that n cannot be factored into two natural numbers each exceeding 1. Neither condition adds anything to the other; they are two formulations of the same requirement. As for condition (iii), it too is a consequence of (i): the only even natural number greater than 1 that has no divisors other than 1 and itself is 2, since every other even number is divisible by 2 and hence has a divisor other than 1 and itself. One might observe that a well-constructed definition should contain only logically independent conditions. The present definition, by stating the same requirement in three equivalent ways, creates the misleading impression that primality involves the satisfaction of three separate tests, when in truth a single test — that n have no divisors other than 1 and n — suffices entirely. The other conditions are not wrong, but their inclusion obscures the simplicity of the concept they are meant to define.

definition: A set G together with a binary operation * constitutes a group if and only if (i) G is closed under * — for all a, b in G, a * b is in G, (ii) * is associative — for all a, b, c in G, (a * b) * c = a * (b * c), and (iii) every element of G has an inverse — for every a in G, there exists b in G such that a * b = b * a = e, where e is an element of G satisfying e * a = a * e = a for all a in G.
conditions: G is closed under the operation
The operation is associative
Every element has an inverse relative to an identity element
counterexample: Consider the set of all non-zero rational numbers under multiplication. The set is closed under multiplication; the operation is associative; and every non-zero rational number has a multiplicative inverse that is also a non-zero rational number. All three conditions are satisfied, and the structure is indeed a group. Now consider the set of all positive rational numbers less than 1 under multiplication. The set is closed (the product of two positive rationals less than 1 is again a positive rational less than 1), and multiplication is associative. But there is no identity element in this set — the multiplicative identity is 1, which is not less than 1 — and consequently no element has an inverse in the required sense. Condition (iii) fails. The definition correctly identifies this as not a group. Yet the definition's formulation of condition (iii) embeds the existence of the identity element within the statement of the inverse condition, rather than listing it as a separate requirement, which can obscure the fact that the failure is one of identity rather than of inverses per se.
missing_condition: The definition embeds the requirement of an identity element within the inverse condition rather than stating it as an independent requirement, which obscures the diagnosis of structures that fail to be groups precisely because they lack an identity element rather than because their elements lack inverses.
passage: One might define a group as a set closed under an associative binary operation in which every element possesses an inverse. This definition is standard in algebra and captures the essential structure of symmetry groups, permutation groups, and number groups. Yet a case which reveals a subtle insufficiency in the formulation arises when one considers the requirement of an identity element. Condition (iii) mentions the identity element e as part of the definition of an inverse — an element b is an inverse of a if a * b = b * a = e — but does not list the existence of e as a separate condition. In practice, the existence of the identity is a consequence of closure, associativity, and the existence of inverses, so no logical error is introduced. Nevertheless, the embedding of the identity within the inverse condition can obscure the diagnosis of structures that fail to be groups. Consider the set of positive rational numbers less than 1 under multiplication. The set is closed: the product of two positive rationals less than 1 is again a positive rational less than 1. Multiplication is associative. But the multiplicative identity — the number 1 — does not belong to the set, and consequently no element has an inverse in the set. One might say that condition (iii) fails, but the root of the failure is the absence of the identity element, not the absence of inverses in any deeper sense. If 1 were included, every element except 1 would still lack an inverse within the set (since for q < 1, the reciprocal 1/q exceeds 1). One might observe that a definition which treats the identity as a precondition for the existence of inverses, rather than folding it into the inverse requirement, would more perspicuously diagnose such failures.

definition: A function T from a vector space V to a vector space W is linear if and only if (i) T preserves addition — T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v) for all u, v in V, (ii) T preserves scalar multiplication — T(cv) = cT(v) for all scalars c and all v in V, and (iii) T maps the zero vector of V to the zero vector of W.
conditions: T preserves addition
T preserves scalar multiplication
T maps the zero vector to the zero vector
counterexample: Consider any function T satisfying conditions (i) and (ii). Setting v = 0 in condition (ii), we obtain T(c * 0) = cT(0), i.e. T(0) = cT(0) for all scalars c. This implies T(0) = 0. Condition (iii) is a logical consequence of conditions (i) and (ii) and is therefore redundant. Alternatively, setting u = v = 0 in condition (i), we obtain T(0 + 0) = T(0) + T(0), whence T(0) = 0. The definition presents three conditions, but only two are logically independent.
passage: One might define a linear function between vector spaces by requiring that it preserve addition, preserve scalar multiplication, and map the zero vector to the zero vector. This definition is often presented in introductory courses as though it imposed three independent constraints. Yet a case which reveals its redundancy may be exhibited with a few lines of algebra. Suppose T satisfies conditions (i) and (ii). Setting v equal to the zero vector in condition (ii), one obtains T(c * 0) = cT(0) for every scalar c. Since c * 0 = 0, this gives T(0) = cT(0) for all c, which is possible only if T(0) = 0. Condition (iii) follows from (ii) alone. Alternatively, setting both u and v equal to the zero vector in condition (i), one obtains T(0 + 0) = T(0) + T(0), i.e. T(0) = T(0) + T(0), which again yields T(0) = 0. Condition (iii) follows from (i) alone. The definition presents three conditions as though they jointly characterise linearity, when in fact any two of conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) — indeed, conditions (i) and (ii) by themselves — suffice. One might observe that a well-constructed definition should not include conditions that are logical consequences of the others, for their inclusion suggests that the concept is more complex than it is and invites the student to search for cases in which (iii) fails while (i) and (ii) hold — a search that must end in frustration, since no such case exists.

definition: A collection C constitutes a well-defined set if and only if (i) for every object x, it is determinately true or false that x belongs to C, (ii) C is specified by a property that all and only its members satisfy, and (iii) C is itself an object that can serve as a member of other collections.
conditions: Membership in C is determinate for every object
C is specified by a property satisfied by all and only its members
C can be a member of other collections
counterexample: Consider the collection R of all sets that do not contain themselves as members — that is, R is specified by the property that x belongs to R if and only if x does not belong to x. For every set x, the property 'x is not a member of x' appears to be determinately true or false, so condition (i) is satisfied. The collection is specified by a property satisfied by all and only its members, so condition (ii) is satisfied. And we may suppose that R is itself an object capable of membership in other collections, so condition (iii) is satisfied. Yet the resulting collection leads to a paradox: if R belongs to R, then by the defining property R does not belong to R; and if R does not belong to R, then by the defining property it does belong to R.
missing_condition: The definition fails to impose any restriction on which properties may be used to specify sets, and thereby admits properties — such as the property of non-self-membership — that give rise to paradoxes, revealing that unrestricted comprehension is inconsistent with the notion of a well-defined collection.
passage: One might propose that a well-defined set is a collection whose membership is determinate for every object, which is specified by a property satisfied by all and only its members, and which can itself serve as a member of other collections. This definition is a natural formalisation of the naive concept of a set as a collection determined by a defining property. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is the collection of all sets that are not members of themselves — the collection that figured in Russell's celebrated paradox. The property 'x is not a member of x' appears to yield a determinate answer for every set: either a set belongs to itself or it does not. The collection is specified by this property, and we may suppose it is itself an object capable of membership in other collections. All three conditions are met. But the collection so defined cannot exist without contradiction. If R belongs to itself, then by the defining property it does not belong to itself; if it does not belong to itself, then it does. The paradox reveals that condition (ii) — specification by a property — is too permissive: not every grammatically well-formed property determines a set. One might observe that the definition, by imposing no restriction on which properties may serve as set-specifications, fails to distinguish between properties that genuinely determine collections and properties that engender paradox. The development of axiomatic set theory in the early twentieth century was motivated precisely by the need to restrict the principle of comprehension, a restriction that this definition does not make.

definition: A real-valued function m defined on a collection of subsets of a set X is a measure if and only if (i) m assigns a non-negative value to every set in the collection, (ii) m assigns the value zero to the empty set, and (iii) m is countably additive — for any countable family of pairwise disjoint sets in the collection, m of their union equals the sum of their individual measures.
conditions: m is non-negative
m of the empty set is zero
m is countably additive
counterexample: Consider the counting measure on the natural numbers, which assigns to each finite set its cardinality and to each infinite set the value infinity. This measure satisfies all three conditions: it is non-negative; the empty set has measure zero; and it is countably additive (the cardinality of a union of disjoint sets is the sum of their cardinalities). The definition correctly identifies it as a measure. But now consider the finitely additive measure on all subsets of the natural numbers obtained via an ultrafilter — a so-called Banach measure — which assigns 0 or 1 to every subset, is finitely additive, and assigns 0 to the empty set and 1 to the whole set. This function satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) but fails condition (iii), and is therefore not a measure by the definition's lights. Yet in certain branches of analysis and logic, such finitely additive measures are of fundamental importance. The definition, by insisting on countable additivity, excludes a mathematically significant class of set functions.
missing_condition: The definition imposes countable additivity as a necessary condition for being a measure, thereby excluding finitely additive set functions that are of genuine mathematical interest, and does not acknowledge that the choice between finite and countable additivity is a matter of mathematical convention rather than logical necessity.
passage: One might define a measure as a non-negative, countably additive set function that assigns zero to the empty set. This definition is standard in modern measure theory, and its power is demonstrated by the Lebesgue integral and the rich theory of probability that it supports. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency — or at least the restrictiveness — of this definition arises in the context of finitely additive set functions. Consider a function defined on all subsets of the natural numbers that assigns to each subset either the value 0 or the value 1, is finitely additive (the measure of a union of finitely many disjoint sets equals the sum of their measures), assigns 0 to the empty set, and assigns 1 to the whole set. Such a function can be constructed using an ultrafilter on the natural numbers. It satisfies conditions (i) and (ii): it is non-negative and assigns zero to the empty set. But it is not countably additive, for the natural numbers can be partitioned into countably many singletons, each of measure 0, whose union has measure 1. Condition (iii) fails. The definition therefore excludes this function from the class of measures. Yet finitely additive measures of this kind play an important role in mathematical logic, in the theory of Banach limits, and in certain approaches to probability. One might observe that the definition's insistence on countable additivity reflects a particular choice of mathematical convention — one that is appropriate for the Lebesgue theory but not for all contexts in which the concept of measure is fruitfully employed.

definition: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than 1, (ii) n has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself, and (iii) n is odd.
conditions: n is greater than 1
n has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself
n is odd
counterexample: Consider the number 2. It is greater than 1 and has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Yet 2 is even, and hence fails condition (iii). The definition therefore excludes 2 from the prime numbers. Yet 2 is universally regarded as prime — indeed, it is the only even prime — and its exclusion would create anomalies throughout number theory, for many theorems depend upon counting 2 among the primes.
passage: One might attempt to define primality as follows: a natural number is prime if it is greater than 1, has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself, and is odd. The third condition might seem a harmless addition, for one observes that the overwhelming majority of primes are indeed odd. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is immediately at hand. The number 2 satisfies the first two conditions: it exceeds 1 and has no divisors other than 1 and 2. Yet it is even, and therefore fails the third condition. The definition would exclude 2 from the primes. This exclusion, however, is unacceptable. The number 2 is universally recognised as prime, and its primality is essential to numerous results in arithmetic, including the fundamental theorem that every natural number greater than 1 admits a unique factorisation into primes. It appears that the additional condition of oddness, though satisfied by all primes save one, is not a genuine requirement for primality but an accidental feature of the primes greater than 2.

definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point c if and only if (i) f is defined at c, (ii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists, and (iii) the value of f at c equals the limit of f(x) as x approaches c from the right.
conditions: f is defined at c
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists
The value of f at c equals the limit of f(x) as x approaches c from the right
counterexample: Consider the function defined by f(x) = 0 for x < 0 and f(x) = 1 for x >= 0. At c = 0, f is defined and f(0) = 1. The right-hand limit as x approaches 0 is 1, and suppose we consider the full limit to exist and equal 1 (which it does not, but the definition only checks the right-hand limit). The definition's third condition is met since f(0) equals the right-hand limit. Yet the left-hand limit is 0, and f has a jump discontinuity at 0 that the definition fails to detect.
missing_condition: The definition requires agreement with the right-hand limit only, failing to require that f(c) also equal the left-hand limit, so that functions with jump discontinuities approached from the left may be classified as continuous.
passage: One might define continuity at a point as follows: a function is continuous at c if it is defined at c, the limit of the function as x approaches c exists, and the value at c equals the right-hand limit. This definition captures part of the standard notion — the requirement that the function's value match its limiting behaviour. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with step functions. Consider the function that assigns 0 to every negative real number and 1 to every non-negative real number. At c = 0, the function is defined and takes the value 1. The right-hand limit as x approaches 0 is also 1, so the third condition is satisfied. Yet the left-hand limit as x approaches 0 is 0. The function suffers a jump discontinuity at the origin — it leaps from 0 to 1 — and no analyst would regard it as continuous there. It appears that the definition, by requiring agreement only with the right-hand limit, fails to detect discontinuities that manifest on the left. A satisfactory definition must require that the function's value agree with the full two-sided limit, not merely with the limit from one direction.

definition: An infinite series S = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + ... converges if and only if (i) the terms a_n tend to zero as n tends to infinity, (ii) the partial sums S_n = a_1 + ... + a_n are bounded, and (iii) the terms a_n are eventually all of the same sign.
conditions: The terms a_n tend to zero as n tends to infinity
The partial sums S_n are bounded
The terms a_n are eventually all of the same sign
counterexample: Consider the alternating harmonic series 1 - 1/2 + 1/3 - 1/4 + ... . The terms tend to zero, the partial sums are bounded (they oscillate between values near ln 2), and yet the terms are never eventually all of the same sign, for they alternate in sign indefinitely. The series converges to ln 2 by Leibniz's criterion, yet it violates condition (iii). The definition would therefore classify a convergent series as divergent.
passage: One might attempt to characterise the convergence of an infinite series by requiring that the terms tend to zero, the partial sums are bounded, and the terms are eventually of constant sign. The first two conditions are familiar necessary conditions, and the third might seem a reasonable simplifying assumption. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is well known. The alternating harmonic series, whose terms are 1, -1/2, 1/3, -1/4, and so forth, has terms that tend to zero and partial sums that remain bounded, oscillating in a neighbourhood of the natural logarithm of 2. The series converges by the Leibniz criterion for alternating series. Yet its terms alternate in sign indefinitely and never become eventually positive or eventually negative. The definition, by imposing the requirement that the terms be eventually of constant sign, excludes a large and important class of convergent series — the conditionally convergent alternating series. It appears that the third condition, though satisfied by many familiar convergent series with positive terms, is not a genuine requirement for convergence.

definition: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X is non-empty, (ii) X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets, and (iii) every continuous function from X to the real numbers attains every value between any two values it assumes.
conditions: X is non-empty
X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets
Every continuous function from X to the real numbers attains every value between any two values it assumes
counterexample: Consider the topologist's sine curve, which is the closure in the plane of the set {(x, sin(1/x)) : x > 0}. This space is connected in the standard topological sense — it cannot be partitioned into two disjoint non-empty open sets — and it is non-empty. However, it fails to satisfy the intermediate value property for all continuous functions into the real numbers, since it is connected but not path-connected, and certain continuous real-valued functions on it do not behave as condition (iii) requires. Condition (iii) conflates connectedness with a stronger property.
passage: One might propose that a topological space is connected if it is non-empty, cannot be decomposed into two disjoint non-empty open sets, and satisfies the intermediate value property for every continuous real-valued function defined upon it. The first two conditions constitute the standard definition of connectedness. The third condition — the intermediate value property — holds for all connected subsets of the real line and might seem a natural addition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the strengthened definition arises with the topologist's sine curve: the closure in the Euclidean plane of the graph of sin(1/x) for positive x. This space is connected in the standard sense, for it cannot be partitioned into two disjoint non-empty open sets. Yet its topological structure is such that the third condition imposes a requirement beyond mere connectedness — it demands a form of path-connectedness that the topologist's sine curve does not possess. The definition, by including the intermediate value property as a necessary condition, conflates connectedness with a strictly stronger topological property and thereby excludes spaces that are genuinely connected.

definition: A function f from the natural numbers to the natural numbers is computable if and only if (i) there exists a finite set of instructions that, when followed mechanically, produces f(n) for any input n, (ii) the computation halts after finitely many steps for every input, and (iii) the instructions do not require the use of unbounded memory.
conditions: There exists a finite set of instructions that, when followed mechanically, produces f(n) for any input n
The computation halts after finitely many steps for every input
The instructions do not require the use of unbounded memory
counterexample: Consider the Ackermann function, which is a total computable function that grows faster than any primitive recursive function. It is computed by a finite set of instructions and halts for every input. Yet its computation requires a stack whose depth grows without any fixed bound — for sufficiently large inputs, the memory required exceeds any predetermined limit. Condition (iii) would exclude the Ackermann function from the class of computable functions, yet it is computable by any standard account.
passage: One might define a computable function as one for which there exists a finite set of mechanical instructions that halts for every input and does not require unbounded memory. The first two conditions correspond to the standard notion of computability. The third condition — bounded memory — might seem a practical requirement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by the Ackermann function. This function is defined recursively and is total: it produces a value for every pair of natural-number arguments. It is computable in the standard sense — a Turing machine can calculate its values. Yet the computation of the Ackermann function for large arguments requires a memory stack whose depth grows beyond any fixed bound. For any predetermined memory limit, there exist inputs for which the computation exceeds that limit. The definition, by requiring that the instructions not use unbounded memory, would exclude the Ackermann function from the computable functions. It appears that the third condition conflates computability in principle with computability under resource constraints, and thereby mistakes a practical limitation for a theoretical one.

definition: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) every element of A can be paired with exactly one element of B, (ii) every element of B can be paired with exactly one element of A, and (iii) A and B have the same number of elements when enumerated.
conditions: Every element of A can be paired with exactly one element of B
Every element of B can be paired with exactly one element of A
A and B have the same number of elements when enumerated
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers {1, 2, 3, ...} and the set of even natural numbers {2, 4, 6, ...}. One may construct a bijection by pairing each natural number n with the even number 2n. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet if one attempts to enumerate both sets and compare their sizes, the set of natural numbers appears to have 'more' elements, for the even numbers form a proper subset. Condition (iii), interpreted as requiring that the two enumerations match in length, is either vacuous for infinite sets or misleading.
passage: One might attempt to define equicardinality by requiring that the elements of two sets can be paired one-to-one and that the sets have the same number of elements when enumerated. The first two conditions capture the standard notion of a bijection. The third condition might seem to add nothing, for a bijection already guarantees that the sets are, in the relevant sense, the same size. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency — or rather the incoherence — of the third condition arises with infinite sets. Consider the natural numbers and the even natural numbers. The function that sends each natural number n to the even number 2n is a bijection, so conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet the even numbers form a proper subset of the natural numbers: every even number is a natural number, but not every natural number is even. If 'having the same number of elements when enumerated' is interpreted as requiring that neither set contain elements left over after the pairing, the condition is satisfied. But if it is interpreted as requiring that the sets be of the same finite size, it is inapplicable to infinite sets. It appears that the third condition is either redundant or misleading, and that the notion of equicardinality is fully captured by the existence of a bijection alone.

definition: A formal system S is complete if and only if (i) every well-formed formula of S is either provable or refutable in S, (ii) S is consistent, and (iii) S contains only finitely many axioms.
conditions: Every well-formed formula of S is either provable or refutable in S
S is consistent
S contains only finitely many axioms
counterexample: Consider Presburger arithmetic, the first-order theory of the natural numbers with addition but without multiplication. This system is consistent and complete — every sentence in its language is either provable or refutable. Yet it is axiomatised by an infinite axiom schema (the induction schema generates infinitely many axioms). Condition (iii) would exclude Presburger arithmetic from the class of complete systems, despite its completeness being a well-established theorem.
passage: One might define completeness for a formal system by requiring that every formula be provable or refutable, that the system be consistent, and that it have only finitely many axioms. The first two conditions capture the standard notion of syntactic completeness. The third might be motivated by a desire for simplicity or surveyability. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises with Presburger arithmetic — the theory of the natural numbers under addition alone, without multiplication. Presburger arithmetic is demonstrably complete: every sentence in its language is decided by its axioms. It is consistent. Yet its axiomatisation employs an induction schema that generates infinitely many axioms, one for each formula in the language. The definition, by imposing a finite-axiom requirement, would exclude this paradigmatic example of a complete decidable theory. It appears that the third condition confuses a desirable property of axiom systems — finitude and surveyability — with a requirement for completeness, which is a property of what the system can prove, not of how many axioms it employs.

definition: A set S of natural numbers is decidable if and only if (i) there exists an effective procedure that, given any natural number n, determines whether n belongs to S, (ii) the procedure halts for every input, and (iii) the procedure runs in polynomial time.
conditions: There exists an effective procedure that, given any natural number n, determines whether n belongs to S
The procedure halts for every input
The procedure runs in polynomial time
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers that encode valid proofs in first-order predicate logic. Checking whether a given string is a valid proof is decidable — one need only verify that each step follows from the axioms and rules of inference — and the procedure halts for every input. Yet for sufficiently long proofs, the verification may require time that is exponential in the length of the input. The set is decidable in the standard recursion-theoretic sense, but condition (iii) would exclude it because the procedure need not run in polynomial time.
passage: One might define a decidable set of natural numbers as one for which there exists an effective procedure that halts on every input and runs in polynomial time. The first two conditions correspond to the standard notion of decidability in recursion theory. The third condition — polynomial running time — might seem a natural efficiency requirement.
