All of the first two conditions are satisfied, and 2 is universally recognised as prime — indeed, it is the smallest prime. The third condition excludes it, revealing that the condition of oddness is too restrictive.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously includes the requirement that n be odd. The correct characterisation requires only that n have no positive divisors other than 1 and itself; the parity of n is irrelevant to its primality.
passage: One might attempt to characterise the prime numbers by stipulating that a positive integer greater than 1 is prime if and only if it has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself, cannot be expressed as a product of two integers both exceeding 1, and is odd. The first two conditions are, in substance, equivalent formulations of the standard notion of primality, and the third — the requirement of oddness — might appear to be a harmless addition, since the vast majority of prime numbers are indeed odd. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation is readily available. Consider the integer 2. It has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself: no integer between 1 and 2 divides it. It cannot be expressed as a product of two positive integers both greater than 1, for the only factorisation of 2 into positive integers is 1 times 2. Yet 2 is even. The proposed definition would therefore exclude 2 from the class of primes. This is plainly incorrect, for 2 is universally recognised as the smallest prime number and plays a distinguished role in number theory precisely because it is the only even prime. The error lies in the third condition: oddness is a property that happens to be shared by all primes except one, but it is not constitutive of primality. The definition mistakes a near-universal but accidental feature for an essential one, and in doing so excludes the very number that most sharply illustrates the distinction between the two.

definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point c if and only if (i) f is defined at c, (ii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists, and (iii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c equals f(c).
conditions: f is defined at c
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c equals f(c)
counterexample: This definition is, in fact, the standard characterisation of continuity at a point for real-valued functions on the real line, and no genuine case satisfying all three conditions whilst failing to be continuous can be constructed within this domain. However, consider the function f defined on the rational numbers alone by f(x) = x. This function satisfies a natural analogue of all three conditions at every point in its domain, yet we should hesitate to call it continuous in the fullest sense, for it is defined only on a set riddled with gaps, and the notion of a limit must be interpreted relative to the restricted domain rather than the real line.
missing_condition: The definition, whilst correct for functions on the real line, fails to specify the topological or metric context in which limits are taken — that is, the nature of the domain over which the function is defined. When applied to functions on more exotic domains, the conditions must be interpreted with reference to an appropriate notion of convergence.
passage: The standard characterisation of continuity at a point for a real-valued function on the real line requires three things: that the function be defined at the point, that the limit exist as the argument approaches the point, and that the limit equal the function's value there. This formulation is elegant and, within its intended context, entirely adequate. Yet one might observe that it silently presupposes a particular setting — namely, the real line with its usual metric. Consider the function f defined only on the rational numbers by f(x) = x. At any rational point c, f is defined, the limit of f(x) as x approaches c through the rationals exists and equals c, and the limit equals f(c). All three conditions are satisfied, interpreted relative to the restricted domain. Yet the function is defined on a set that is, in a precise topological sense, full of gaps: between any two rationals lie uncountably many irrationals at which f has no value. Whether we should regard f as continuous depends entirely upon the structure we impose on its domain. If we treat the rationals as a metric subspace of the reals, f is continuous; if we insist that continuity requires the function to be defined on a connected domain, f falls short. The definition, by failing to specify the topological or metric context in which its conditions are to be interpreted, leaves open the question of what counts as a limit and what counts as an approach, and thereby proves insufficient when transported beyond the familiar setting of the real line.

definition: An infinite series S = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + ... converges if and only if (i) the terms a_n tend to zero as n tends to infinity, (ii) the partial sums S_n = a_1 + ... + a_n are bounded, and (iii) the terms a_n are eventually all of the same sign.
conditions: The terms a_n tend to zero as n tends to infinity
The partial sums S_n are bounded
The terms a_n are eventually all of the same sign
counterexample: Consider the harmonic series 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 + ... . The terms 1/n tend to zero. The terms are all positive, hence eventually of the same sign. Yet the partial sums grow without bound — the harmonic series diverges. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied but (ii) is not. Conversely, consider the alternating series 1 - 1/2 + 1/3 - 1/4 + ... . The terms tend to zero, the partial sums are bounded, but the terms are never eventually all of the same sign. Yet this series converges to ln(2). The third condition excludes a convergent series.
missing_condition: The definition is defective in its third condition: the requirement that the terms be eventually of the same sign is neither necessary nor sufficient for convergence. Many convergent series — including all non-trivially alternating series — violate this condition.
passage: One might attempt to characterise the convergence of an infinite series by requiring that the terms tend to zero, the partial sums remain bounded, and the terms are eventually all of the same sign. The first condition is certainly necessary — a series whose terms do not tend to zero cannot converge. The second captures the intuition that a convergent series must not accumulate without limit. The third might appear a natural simplifying assumption, restricting attention to the most tractable class of series. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation is readily found in the elementary theory of series. Consider the alternating harmonic series: 1 minus one-half plus one-third minus one-quarter, and so on. The terms tend to zero; the partial sums are bounded, oscillating about the value ln 2. Yet the terms alternate in sign and are never eventually all positive or all negative. The proposed definition would exclude this series from the class of convergent series, despite the fact that it converges — by the Leibniz criterion and by direct computation — to the natural logarithm of 2. The error lies in the third condition, which mistakes a feature of certain well-behaved series for a requirement of convergence itself. Indeed, some of the most important and interesting convergent series in analysis — including the series for the sine and cosine functions, and a great many Fourier series — involve terms of alternating or irregular sign. The condition of eventual same-signedness is a convenience of exposition, not a constitutive feature of convergence.

definition: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X is non-empty, (ii) X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets, and (iii) every continuous function from X to the real numbers satisfies the intermediate value property.
conditions: X is non-empty
X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets
Every continuous function from X to the real numbers satisfies the intermediate value property
counterexample: Conditions (ii) and (iii) are in fact equivalent for a non-empty topological space, and both correctly characterise connectedness. However, the inclusion of condition (iii) alongside (ii) might lead one to suppose that each condition captures a different aspect of the concept. Consider the topologist's sine curve: the closure in the plane of the graph of sin(1/x) for x > 0. This space is connected — it cannot be written as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets. Yet it is not path-connected, and one might mistakenly expect the intermediate value property to fail for functions defined on it. In fact, the intermediate value property holds, but the space illustrates that connectedness and path-connectedness are distinct notions. The definition, by conflating two equivalent conditions, obscures this important distinction.
missing_condition: The definition, whilst extensionally correct, is redundant: conditions (ii) and (iii) are equivalent for non-empty topological spaces. It would be improved by replacing one of the two equivalent conditions with the strictly stronger requirement of path-connectedness, thereby distinguishing between the two notions that the current formulation conflates.
passage: One might characterise a connected topological space as a non-empty space that cannot be written as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets and in which every continuous real-valued function satisfies the intermediate value property. Each of these conditions captures a genuine aspect of what is meant by connectedness, and one might suppose that listing both provides a more robust characterisation. Yet the relationship between conditions (ii) and (iii) repays closer examination. It is a theorem of elementary topology that, for non-empty spaces, the two conditions are equivalent: a space cannot be partitioned into two disjoint non-empty open sets if and only if every continuous real-valued function on it satisfies the intermediate value property. The definition is therefore redundant — it lists two formulations of the same requirement as though they were independent. This redundancy becomes problematic when one considers the topologist's sine curve, a classic example that illustrates the gap between connectedness and the stronger property of path-connectedness. The closure in the plane of the graph of sin(1/x) for positive x is connected: it cannot be so partitioned, and continuous real-valued functions on it do satisfy the intermediate value property. Yet it is not path-connected — there is no continuous path from a point on the oscillating part to a point on the vertical segment. By listing two equivalent conditions and no others, the definition gives the misleading impression that it has captured everything of importance about the spatial unity of a topological space, when in fact it has merely stated the same thing twice and has entirely overlooked the distinction between connectedness and path-connectedness.

definition: A function f from natural numbers to natural numbers is computable if and only if (i) there exists a finite set of instructions that, when followed mechanically, produces f(n) for every input n, (ii) the set of instructions terminates in a finite number of steps for every input, and (iii) the instructions require no external information beyond the input n.
conditions: There exists a finite set of instructions that, when followed mechanically, produces f(n) for every input n
The set of instructions terminates in a finite number of steps for every input
The instructions require no external information beyond the input n
counterexample: Consider the Busy Beaver function BB(n), which gives the maximum number of steps a halting Turing machine with n states can execute before halting. For every input n, the value BB(n) is a well-defined natural number. In principle, one could enumerate all n-state Turing machines, simulate each, and record which halting machines take the most steps. This procedure uses no external information beyond n. For any specific n, the procedure terminates in finitely many steps, since there are only finitely many n-state machines. Yet the Busy Beaver function is not computable — it grows faster than any computable function. The difficulty is that the procedure described is not uniform: there is no single finite set of instructions that works for all n, because determining which n-state machines halt is itself undecidable.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the same finite set of instructions work uniformly for all inputs — that a single algorithm handle every n, rather than a potentially different procedure for each particular value of n.
passage: One might characterise a computable function as one for which there exists a finite set of mechanical instructions that produces the function's value for every input, terminates in finitely many steps, and requires no information beyond the input itself. This captures the essential idea of effective calculability: the process is mechanical, finite, and self-contained. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the theory of computation itself. Consider the Busy Beaver function, which assigns to each natural number n the maximum number of steps that a halting Turing machine with n states can execute before halting. For any particular value of n, the value of the function is a well-defined natural number, and one could in principle determine it by enumerating all n-state Turing machines, simulating each to see which ones halt, and recording the maximum number of steps among those that do. This procedure, for any given n, terminates in finitely many steps — there are only finitely many n-state machines — and requires no external information beyond the input. Yet the Busy Beaver function is not computable. It grows faster than any computable function, and no single algorithm can produce its values for all inputs. The difficulty is that the description given above is not a single finite set of instructions that works uniformly for every n; for each n, the procedure of determining which machines halt requires solving the halting problem restricted to n-state machines, and there is no uniform method for doing so. What the definition fails to require is that the finite set of instructions be the same for every input — that a single algorithm handle all cases, rather than a potentially different ad hoc procedure for each particular value of the argument.

definition: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function from A to B that is injective, (ii) there exists a function from B to A that is injective, and (iii) there exists a function from A to B that is surjective.
conditions: There exists a function from A to B that is injective
There exists a function from B to A that is injective
There exists a function from A to B that is surjective
counterexample: Consider A = the set of all real numbers and B = the set of all real numbers in the open interval (0, 1). The function f(x) = 1/(1 + e^(-x)) maps A injectively into B. The inclusion map from B into A is trivially injective. And f is surjective onto B. All three conditions are satisfied, and indeed A and B do have the same cardinality. In this instance the definition gives the correct answer. But the conditions are redundant: the Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder theorem guarantees that conditions (i) and (ii) alone suffice to establish equal cardinality, making condition (iii) unnecessary. Conversely, conditions (i) and (iii) together already imply that the function in (i) can be modified to a bijection. The definition piles up conditions that overlap without capturing the single essential requirement.
missing_condition: The definition is correct but unnecessarily complex. The essential requirement is the existence of a single bijection — a function that is both injective and surjective — between A and B. The three separate conditions listed are individually weaker than bijectivity and collectively redundant in various combinations.
passage: One might attempt to characterise equal cardinality between two sets by requiring the existence of an injection from each to the other and, in addition, the existence of a surjection from the first to the second. Each of these conditions captures a genuine aspect of the size-comparison between sets: an injection from A to B shows that A is no larger than B, an injection from B to A shows that B is no larger than A, and a surjection from A to B shows that B is no larger than A in a somewhat different sense. Yet the proliferation of conditions obscures what is essentially a single requirement. The Cantor-Bernstein-Schroeder theorem establishes that the existence of injections in both directions already guarantees the existence of a bijection, rendering condition (iii) redundant. Conversely, the existence of an injection and a surjection from A to B — conditions (i) and (iii) — can themselves be combined to construct a bijection under the axiom of choice. The definition, whilst not strictly incorrect in the cases it admits, is structurally deficient in that it decomposes a single, unified concept — the existence of a one-to-one correspondence between the two sets — into three overlapping fragments, none of which individually states the essential requirement and which collectively introduce redundancy without additional content. A more perspicuous characterisation would simply require the existence of a bijection from A to B: a single function that is both injective and surjective. This captures in one stroke what the three conditions laboriously approximate, and it makes plain the structural unity of the concept of equal cardinality.

definition: A metric space (X, d) is complete if and only if (i) every Cauchy sequence in X has a limit, (ii) that limit belongs to X, and (iii) X contains no isolated points.
conditions: Every Cauchy sequence in X has a limit
That limit belongs to X
X contains no isolated points
counterexample: Consider the metric space consisting of a single point, say X = {0}, with the usual metric. Every sequence in X is the constant sequence 0, which is trivially Cauchy and converges to 0 in X. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet the single point 0 is an isolated point — indeed, it is the only point. The proposed definition would exclude this space from the class of complete metric spaces. Yet {0} is complete: every Cauchy sequence converges, and the limit lies in the space. More generally, any finite discrete metric space is complete despite consisting entirely of isolated points.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously includes the requirement that X contain no isolated points. Completeness concerns only the convergence of Cauchy sequences within the space and has nothing to do with the isolation or accumulation of points.
passage: One might characterise a complete metric space as one in which every Cauchy sequence has a limit belonging to the space, and which contains no isolated points. The first two conditions express the fundamental idea: sequences that ought to converge — those whose terms draw arbitrarily close to one another — do in fact converge to a point within the space. The third condition might appear to ensure a certain richness of structure, excluding degenerate or trivially discrete spaces. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation is immediately available. Consider the metric space consisting of a single point — say X = {0} — endowed with the usual metric. The only sequence in this space is the constant sequence 0, 0, 0, ..., which is trivially Cauchy and converges to 0, a point of X. Conditions (i) and (ii) are manifestly satisfied. Yet 0 is an isolated point: there is no other point within any positive distance of it. The proposed definition would therefore deny that this space is complete. This is plainly wrong: {0} is a paradigmatic example of a complete metric space. Indeed, any finite metric space is complete, since every sequence in a finite set is eventually constant and hence converges. The error lies in the third condition. The absence of isolated points is a topological property that has nothing whatever to do with the completeness of a metric space. Completeness is solely a matter of whether Cauchy sequences converge within the space; the spatial arrangement of the points — whether they cluster or stand apart — is entirely irrelevant.

definition: A set S of natural numbers is decidable if and only if (i) there exists an algorithm that, given any natural number n, halts and outputs 'yes' if n is in S, (ii) the algorithm halts and outputs 'no' if n is not in S, and (iii) the algorithm runs in polynomial time.
conditions: There exists an algorithm that halts and outputs 'yes' for every n in S
The algorithm halts and outputs 'no' for every n not in S
The algorithm runs in polynomial time
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers that encode valid proofs in first-order Peano arithmetic. Given any natural number n, one can check whether n encodes a valid proof by mechanically verifying each step against the axioms and inference rules. This procedure halts for every input, outputting 'yes' or 'no' as appropriate. The set is decidable. Yet the verification procedure may, for proofs of great length and complexity, require time that is exponential or worse in the size of the input. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) — polynomial running time — is not. The set is nonetheless decidable; it is merely not efficiently decidable.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously conflates decidability with efficient decidability by requiring polynomial running time. The standard notion of decidability requires only that the algorithm halt for every input and give the correct answer — the time complexity of the algorithm is irrelevant.
passage: One might propose that a set of natural numbers is decidable if there exists an algorithm that halts on every input, correctly reporting membership or non-membership, and runs in polynomial time. The first two conditions capture the core of decidability: for every natural number, the algorithm terminates and delivers a definitive answer. The third condition — polynomial running time — might appear to be a natural requirement ensuring that the decision procedure is not merely theoretical but practically executable. Yet a case which reveals that this additional requirement is too strong may be constructed from the theory of formal proofs. Consider the set of natural numbers that encode valid proofs in first-order Peano arithmetic. Given any candidate number n, one may check whether it encodes a valid proof by mechanically verifying each line of the purported proof against the axioms and rules of inference. This verification procedure halts for every input and gives the correct answer: 'yes' if n encodes a valid proof, 'no' otherwise. The set is decidable in the standard sense. Yet the verification of very long and complex proofs may require time that grows exponentially — or worse — with the size of the encoding. The procedure does not run in polynomial time. The proposed definition would therefore exclude this set from the class of decidable sets, despite the fact that a terminating, correct decision procedure exists. The error lies in the third condition, which conflates decidability — the mere existence of an algorithm that halts and correctly classifies every input — with the far more restrictive notion of efficient decidability. In computability theory, the time required by the algorithm is irrelevant to the question of decidability; what matters is only that it halts and answers correctly.

definition: A function f from a metric space (X, d_X) to a metric space (Y, d_Y) is uniformly continuous if and only if (i) f is continuous at every point of X, (ii) for every epsilon > 0 there exists delta > 0 such that d_X(x, y) < delta implies d_Y(f(x), f(y)) < epsilon for all x, y in X, and (iii) f maps bounded subsets of X to bounded subsets of Y.
conditions: f is continuous at every point of X
For every epsilon > 0 there exists delta > 0 such that d_X(x, y) < delta implies d_Y(f(x), f(y)) < epsilon for all x, y in X
f maps bounded subsets of X to bounded subsets of Y
counterexample: Consider the function f(x) = x^2 from the real line to the real line. This function is continuous at every point. It maps bounded subsets to bounded subsets: if |x| is at most M, then x^2 is at most M^2. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Yet f is not uniformly continuous on the real line, for as x grows large, the oscillation of f over intervals of fixed length grows without bound — no single delta works for all x. Conversely, condition (ii) alone already characterises uniform continuity, making conditions (i) and (iii) redundant. The definition conflates three distinct properties, only one of which is the concept being defined.
missing_condition: The definition is defective in its inclusion of conditions (i) and (iii): condition (ii) alone is the standard characterisation of uniform continuity and implies both of the others. Condition (iii), moreover, is not implied by uniform continuity in general — the identity function on an unbounded space is uniformly continuous but does not convert all bounded sets to bounded sets in any stronger sense than the trivial one.
passage: One might attempt to characterise uniform continuity by requiring that a function be continuous at every point, that it satisfy the epsilon-delta condition with a delta depending only on epsilon and not on the particular point, and that it map bounded sets to bounded sets. The second condition is, in fact, the standard definition of uniform continuity, and the first and third might appear to be natural supplements. Yet the relationship among these three conditions repays careful analysis. Consider the function f(x) = x^2 on the real line. It is continuous at every point — condition (i) is satisfied. It maps bounded sets to bounded sets — if the inputs lie in an interval of length M, the outputs lie in an interval of length at most M^2 plus a constant. Condition (iii) is satisfied. Yet f is not uniformly continuous on the whole real line: for any fixed delta, the difference |f(x) - f(y)| can be made arbitrarily large by choosing x and y close together but both very large. Condition (ii) fails. The example shows that conditions (i) and (iii) do not jointly entail (ii). Conversely, condition (ii) by itself implies condition (i), since uniform continuity is a strictly stronger property than pointwise continuity. The definition thus has a double defect: it pads the characterisation with conditions that are either redundant or insufficient, and in doing so it obscures the sharp distinction between the global, point-independent character of uniform continuity and the local, point-by-point character of ordinary continuity.

definition: A problem P is undecidable if and only if (i) there exists no Turing machine that halts on every input and correctly solves P, (ii) P can be stated in the language of first-order logic, and (iii) P concerns the behaviour of Turing machines.
conditions: There exists no Turing machine that halts on every input and correctly solves P
P can be stated in the language of first-order logic
P concerns the behaviour of Turing machines
counterexample: Consider the problem of determining, given a Diophantine equation (a polynomial equation with integer coefficients), whether it has a solution in the integers. This is Hilbert's tenth problem. Matiyasevich, building on work of Davis, Putnam, and Robinson, showed that this problem is undecidable. Condition (i) is satisfied: no Turing machine can solve it for all inputs. Yet the problem concerns integer solutions to polynomial equations, not the behaviour of Turing machines. Condition (iii) fails. The proposed definition would therefore exclude Hilbert's tenth problem from the class of undecidable problems, despite its being one of the most celebrated examples of undecidability.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously restricts undecidability to problems about Turing machines and to problems expressible in first-order logic. Undecidability is a property of any decision problem for which no total Turing machine gives correct answers on all inputs, regardless of the problem's subject matter or its logical formulation.
passage: One might propose that a problem is undecidable if no Turing machine halts on every input and correctly solves it, the problem can be stated in first-order logic, and the problem concerns the behaviour of Turing machines. The first condition correctly captures the essence of undecidability. The second and third, however, introduce restrictions that have no place in the general concept. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation is provided by one of the most celebrated results in twentieth-century mathematics. Hilbert's tenth problem asks: given an arbitrary polynomial equation with integer coefficients, is there a method for determining whether it has a solution in the integers? The work of Davis, Putnam, Robinson, and Matiyasevich established that no such method exists — the problem is undecidable. Condition (i) is plainly satisfied. Yet the problem has nothing whatsoever to do with the behaviour of Turing machines; it concerns integer solutions to polynomial equations, a topic firmly within number theory and algebra. Condition (iii) fails. Furthermore, whilst the problem can be formalised in first-order logic, this formalisability is incidental to its undecidability; undecidable problems arise in contexts where first-order formulation is awkward or unnatural, such as the word problem for finitely presented groups. The definition, by imposing conditions (ii) and (iii), mistakes the historical context in which undecidability was first discovered — the analysis of computation and formal logic — for the essential nature of the concept itself. Undecidability is a property of decision problems as such, regardless of their subject matter or the logical formalism in which they happen to be stated.

definition: A positive integer n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n is not divisible by any positive integer other than one and itself, and (iii) n can be expressed as the sum of two positive integers in more than one way.
conditions: n is greater than one
n is not divisible by any positive integer other than one and itself
n can be expressed as the sum of two positive integers in more than one way
counterexample: Consider the integer two. It is greater than one, and it is not divisible by any positive integer other than one and itself, so the first two conditions are satisfied. However, two can be expressed as the sum of two positive integers in exactly one way, namely as one plus one. Thus condition (iii) fails for two, even though two is indisputably a prime number. The definition wrongly excludes two from the class of primes.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is spurious: the capacity to be expressed as a sum of two positive integers in more than one way has no bearing upon primality and serves only to exclude the number two, which is a perfectly legitimate prime. The definition ought to consist of conditions (i) and (ii) alone.
passage: One might propose that a positive integer is prime if it is greater than one, is divisible only by one and itself, and can be expressed as the sum of two positive integers in more than one way. The first two conditions capture the classical notion of primality, whilst the third might seem an innocent addition, perhaps motivated by a desire to ensure that primes possess a certain richness of additive structure. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis concerns the number two. Two is greater than one, and it is not divisible by any positive integer other than one and itself, so the first two conditions are met. However, two can be expressed as the sum of two positive integers in exactly one way: as one plus one. There is no other pair of positive integers summing to two. Thus condition (iii) fails for two, notwithstanding the fact that two is universally recognised as the smallest prime. One might observe that the addition of the third condition has introduced a requirement that is not merely unnecessary but actively harmful to the analysis, for it excludes from the class of primes a number that every competent mathematician would include. It appears that the definition, by imposing a condition on the additive decomposability of the integer, introduces a constraint wholly unrelated to the divisibility structure that primality concerns. The correct analysis requires only the first two conditions.

definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point c if and only if (i) f is defined at c, (ii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists, and (iii) for every sequence of rational numbers converging to c, the sequence of function values converges to f(c).
conditions: f is defined at c
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists
For every sequence of rational numbers converging to c, the sequence of function values converges to f(c)
counterexample: Let us imagine a function f defined on the real numbers as follows: f(x) equals zero when x is rational and equals one when x is irrational. Consider the point c equal to zero. The function is defined at zero, where f(0) equals zero. For every sequence of rational numbers converging to zero, the corresponding function values are all zero, which converges to f(0). The limit of f(x) as x approaches zero through rational values exists and equals zero. Yet the limit of f(x) as x approaches zero does not exist in the full sense, for sequences of irrational numbers converging to zero yield function values all equal to one. Thus condition (ii) fails. However, if we modify the function so that the limit does exist — for instance, if f is the indicator function of the rationals altered at zero alone — we can construct cases where condition (iii) is met via rational sequences but the function is not continuous because irrational sequences behave differently. The analysis restricts its attention to rational sequences and thereby overlooks the behaviour of f along irrational approaches.
missing_condition: The definition restricts condition (iii) to sequences of rational numbers, failing to require that f(x_n) converge to f(c) for every sequence x_n converging to c, whether rational or irrational. Continuity at a point demands agreement along all approaching sequences, not merely rational ones.
passage: One might define continuity of a real-valued function at a point by requiring that the function be defined there, that its limit exist, and that for every sequence of rational numbers converging to the point, the sequence of function values converge to the function's value at that point. The first two conditions are standard, and the third might appear to capture the sequential characterisation of continuity, since the rationals are dense in the reals. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Let f be defined so that f(x) equals x when x is rational and f(x) equals negative x when x is irrational. Consider the point c equal to zero. The function is defined at c, with f(0) equal to zero. The limit of f(x) as x approaches zero exists and equals zero, since both the rational and irrational branches approach zero. For every sequence of rational numbers converging to zero, the function values equal the sequence terms themselves, which converge to zero, satisfying condition (iii). All three conditions are met at c equal to zero. Now consider the point c equal to one. The function is defined there, with f(1) equal to one. For every sequence of rational numbers converging to one, the function values converge to one, which equals f(1). But for a sequence of irrational numbers converging to one, the function values converge to negative one, which differs from f(1). The limit of f(x) as x approaches one does not exist, so condition (ii) fails. Yet one might observe that the real deficiency lies in condition (iii): by restricting its attention to rational sequences, the definition can be satisfied even when the function behaves erratically along irrational approaches. A correct analysis of continuity must require convergence along all sequences approaching c, not merely the rational ones.

definition: An infinite series the sum of a_n from n equals one to infinity converges if and only if (i) the terms a_n tend to zero as n tends to infinity, (ii) the partial sums form a bounded sequence, and (iii) the terms a_n are eventually all of the same sign.
conditions: The terms a_n tend to zero as n tends to infinity
The partial sums form a bounded sequence
The terms a_n are eventually all of the same sign
counterexample: Consider the alternating harmonic series, the sum of (-1)^(n+1)/n. The terms tend to zero as n tends to infinity, so condition (i) is satisfied. The partial sums oscillate between values that remain bounded, so condition (ii) is met. Yet the terms alternate in sign indefinitely and are never eventually all of the same sign, so condition (iii) fails. Nevertheless, the alternating harmonic series converges, by Leibniz's criterion, to the natural logarithm of two. The definition wrongly excludes a convergent series.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is an unnecessary restriction that excludes convergent series with terms of alternating sign, such as the alternating harmonic series. The genuine conditions for convergence do not require eventual sign constancy but rather that the partial sums form a Cauchy sequence.
passage: One might propose that an infinite series converges if its terms tend to zero, its partial sums are bounded, and its terms are eventually all of the same sign. The first condition eliminates the most obvious cases of divergence; the second prevents the partial sums from growing without bound; and the third might seem a natural additional safeguard. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the alternating harmonic series, namely the sum of (-1)^(n+1)/n for n from one to infinity. The terms of this series tend to zero, satisfying condition (i). The partial sums oscillate within a bounded interval, satisfying condition (ii). But the terms alternate in sign indefinitely: no matter how far along the sequence one proceeds, subsequent terms continue to change sign. Condition (iii) therefore fails. Nevertheless, the series converges, as Leibniz's criterion establishes, to the natural logarithm of two. One might observe that the definition has imposed a condition — eventual sign constancy — that is by no means necessary for convergence. Many important convergent series in analysis have terms of indefinitely alternating sign, and their convergence is guaranteed not by any uniformity of sign but by the manner in which the partial sums approach a limiting value. The correct characterisation of convergence requires that the sequence of partial sums satisfy the Cauchy criterion, a condition that makes no reference to the signs of the individual terms.

definition: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X is non-empty, (ii) X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets, and (iii) every continuous function from X to the real numbers attains every value between any two values it assumes.
conditions: X is non-empty
X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets
Every continuous function from X to the real numbers attains every value between any two values it assumes
counterexample: Consider any non-empty connected topological space X. Conditions (i) and (ii) are the standard characterisation of connectedness, and condition (iii) is the intermediate value property. It is well known that every connected space satisfies the intermediate value property, so all three conditions hold for any connected space, and the definition is not refuted in that direction. However, suppose we consider the converse: let X be the topologist's sine curve, the closure in the plane of the set of points (x, sin(1/x)) for x in (0,1]. This space is connected and satisfies all three conditions. Now consider instead the graph of a function f from the rationals to the reals that satisfies the intermediate value property by a special construction: for instance, a function on a disconnected space that satisfies (iii) vacuously on each component. All three conditions can be satisfied by a space that, upon closer inspection, fails to be path-connected, showing that condition (iii) adds nothing beyond what (ii) already provides for connected spaces.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is redundant: it is a theorem that every connected space satisfies the intermediate value property, so (iii) adds no content beyond what (i) and (ii) already guarantee. The definition is not wrong but is logically deficient in including a consequence of the other conditions as though it were an independent requirement.
passage: One might characterise a connected topological space as one that is non-empty, cannot be decomposed into two disjoint non-empty open sets, and is such that every continuous real-valued function on it satisfies the intermediate value property. The first two conditions express the standard topological definition of connectedness, and the third might seem a valuable supplement, capturing an important analytical consequence. Yet a closer examination reveals that the third condition adds nothing that the first two do not already entail. It is a well-established theorem that if a topological space is non-empty and cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets, then every continuous function from the space to the reals satisfies the intermediate value property. Condition (iii) is therefore a logical consequence of conditions (i) and (ii), not an independent requirement. One might observe that a definition whose conditions are not logically independent is, in an important sense, deficient — not because it includes anything false, but because it misrepresents the logical structure of the concept being defined. A person who takes the intermediate value property to be an additional requirement, beyond connectedness in the topological sense, misunderstands the relationship between these notions. The correct analysis of connectedness requires precisely conditions (i) and (ii); the intermediate value property is a theorem that follows from them, not a further stipulation.

definition: A function f from the natural numbers to the natural numbers is computable if and only if (i) there exists a finite set of instructions that, when followed mechanically, produces f(n) for any input n, (ii) the set of instructions halts on every input, and (iii) the function f is total — that is, f(n) is defined for every natural number n.
conditions: There exists a finite set of instructions that, when followed mechanically, produces f(n) for any input n
The set of instructions halts on every input
The function f is total
counterexample: Consider the busy beaver function, which assigns to each natural number n the maximum number of ones that an n-state Turing machine can write on an initially blank tape before halting. This function is total: for each n, the maximum exists and is well defined. It is by no means computable, however. There is no finite set of instructions — no Turing machine, no algorithm — that can compute the busy beaver function for all inputs. The function grows faster than any computable function, and its values are in general uncomputable. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are trivially satisfied by the function itself (it is total and, being a mathematical function, does not 'halt' in any procedural sense), but condition (i) fails. The difficulty is that conditions (ii) and (iii) describe properties of f as a function but do not constrain the existence of the requisite instructions. When the conditions are taken conjunctively, the definition permits a reading on which a total function with no computing procedure is erroneously classified if conditions (ii) and (iii) are verified independently of (i).
missing_condition: The definition's conditions are not genuinely independent: conditions (ii) and (iii) restate properties that already follow from condition (i) when properly understood. The real deficiency is that the definition provides no criterion for distinguishing functions for which the requisite instructions exist from those for which they do not, leaving computability without effective content beyond the bare assertion that an algorithm exists.
passage: One might define a computable function as a total function from the natural numbers to the natural numbers for which there exists a finite set of instructions that halts on every input and produces the correct value. This characterisation appears to capture the essential features of computability: the existence of a mechanical procedure, guaranteed termination, and totality. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis concerns the busy beaver function, which assigns to each natural number n the maximum number of ones that an n-state Turing machine can write on a blank tape before halting. The busy beaver function is total: for each n, the maximum is well defined. It is a function from the natural numbers to the natural numbers in the requisite sense. Yet it is not computable. No Turing machine, no finite set of instructions, can produce the busy beaver values for all inputs. One might observe that the difficulty lies in the structure of the definition itself. Conditions (ii) and (iii) — halting on every input and totality — are properties of the function considered as a mathematical object, and they are automatically satisfied by any total function whether computable or not. The substantive content of computability resides entirely in condition (i), the existence of the finite instructions. But the definition provides no means of verifying or establishing this existence beyond the bare assertion. In this respect, the definition is less an analysis of computability than a restatement of the concept it purports to define, for it offers no criterion by which one might determine, for a given total function, whether the requisite instructions exist.

definition: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function from A to B that assigns to each element of A a distinct element of B, (ii) every element of B is assigned to by some element of A, and (iii) A and B have the same number of proper subsets.
conditions: There exists a function from A to B that assigns to each element of A a distinct element of B
Every element of B is assigned to by some element of A
A and B have the same number of proper subsets
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers and the set of even natural numbers. There exists an injective function from the naturals to the evens — namely, the function that sends n to 2n — and this function is surjective onto the evens. Conditions (i) and (ii) are therefore satisfied, establishing a bijection between the two sets. Yet the number of proper subsets of the natural numbers and the number of proper subsets of the even numbers are both uncountably infinite, and while in the sense of cardinality these collections of subsets have the same size, condition (iii) invokes a notion — the 'number' of proper subsets — that is already equivalent to comparing the power sets and thus presupposes the very concept of equal cardinality that the definition is meant to analyse.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is circular: determining whether A and B have the same number of proper subsets requires a prior notion of equinumerosity between the power sets of A and B, which in turn depends upon the concept of equal cardinality. The correct analysis of equicardinality requires only conditions (i) and (ii), which together constitute the existence of a bijection.
passage: One might propose that two sets have the same cardinality if there exists an injective function from one to the other that is also surjective, and if the two sets have the same number of proper subsets. The first two conditions capture the classical notion of a bijection, whilst the third might seem to add a useful structural check: sets that are truly of the same size ought, one might think, to possess equally many proper subsets. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by considering the set of natural numbers and the set of even natural numbers. There exists a bijection between these two sets — the function sending each natural number n to 2n — so conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. As for condition (iii), both sets are countably infinite, and each has uncountably many proper subsets; in the relevant sense, they possess the same 'number' of proper subsets. All three conditions are met, and the definition correctly classifies this pair. One might observe, however, that condition (iii) is not merely redundant but circular. To determine whether A and B have the same number of proper subsets is to compare the cardinalities of their power sets, which presupposes the very concept of equal cardinality that the definition sets out to analyse. The definition thus relies upon the notion it purports to define. It appears that the correct analysis of equicardinality requires only the existence of a bijection — conditions (i) and (ii) — and that condition (iii), far from strengthening the definition, introduces a vicious circularity.

definition: A metric space (X, d) is complete if and only if (i) every Cauchy sequence in X has a limit in X, (ii) X contains all of its limit points, and (iii) every bounded subset of X has a least upper bound in X.
conditions: Every Cauchy sequence in X has a limit in X
X contains all of its limit points
Every bounded subset of X has a least upper bound in X
counterexample: Consider the set of real numbers with the discrete metric, in which d(x, y) equals one when x differs from y and zero when they are equal. In this metric space, a sequence is Cauchy if and only if it is eventually constant, and every eventually constant sequence converges to its eventual value, which belongs to X. So condition (i) is satisfied: the space is complete. Condition (ii) is also satisfied, since in the discrete metric every subset is closed and hence contains all its limit points. Now consider condition (iii). The interval from zero to one is a bounded subset of this space (all distances are at most one), and its least upper bound, one, does belong to X. Yet the notion of a least upper bound presupposes a total ordering on X compatible with the metric, and in general metric spaces there is no such ordering. The definition applies the concept of a least upper bound — an order-theoretic notion — to a setting where it may be meaningless.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) introduces an order-theoretic requirement — the existence of least upper bounds — that is foreign to the purely metric concept of completeness. Completeness in the sense of metric spaces concerns the convergence of Cauchy sequences and has nothing to do with order structure. The condition is either inapplicable or, when applicable, constitutes a distinct and stronger property.
passage: One might define a complete metric space as one in which every Cauchy sequence converges, every limit point belongs to the space, and every bounded subset has a least upper bound within the space. The first condition is the standard characterisation of completeness for metric spaces. The second captures the intuition that the space should not be missing any of its boundary points. The third might seem to add a further guarantee of 'completeness' in an intuitive sense. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis concerns general metric spaces in which no natural ordering exists. Consider the Euclidean plane with the usual metric. This space is complete in the standard sense: every Cauchy sequence converges. It contains all of its limit points. Yet the notion of a least upper bound requires a total ordering, and the plane admits no canonical total order compatible with its metric structure. Condition (iii) is therefore inapplicable to one of the most familiar complete metric spaces. One might observe that the definition has conflated two distinct notions of completeness: metric completeness, which concerns the convergence of Cauchy sequences, and order completeness, which concerns the existence of least upper bounds. These notions coincide for the real line, where both the metric and the order are in harmony, but they diverge in more general settings. It appears that condition (iii) introduces an order-theoretic requirement foreign to the purely metric concept, and a correct analysis of metric completeness should consist of condition (i) alone, with condition (ii) following as a consequence.

definition: A set S of natural numbers is decidable if and only if (i) there exists an effective procedure that, given any natural number n, terminates and outputs 'yes' if n belongs to S, (ii) the same procedure outputs 'no' if n does not belong to S, and (iii) the number of steps required by the procedure is bounded by a polynomial function of n.
conditions: There exists an effective procedure that terminates and outputs 'yes' if n belongs to S
The same procedure outputs 'no' if n does not belong to S
The number of steps required by the procedure is bounded by a polynomial function of n
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers that encode valid proofs in first-order Peano arithmetic. Given any natural number n, one can effectively check whether n encodes a valid proof: verify that each line follows from the axioms or from previous lines by the rules of inference. This procedure terminates for every input, outputting 'yes' if n encodes a valid proof and 'no' otherwise. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, and the set is decidable in the standard sense. Yet the number of steps required to verify a proof of length n may grow exponentially with n, not polynomially. Condition (iii) fails. The definition would classify this set as undecidable, even though it is decidable by every standard account.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) imposes a complexity constraint — polynomial time — that is foreign to the concept of decidability. Decidability requires only the existence of an effective procedure that always terminates with the correct answer; it places no bound on the number of steps. The definition conflates decidability with membership in the complexity class P.
passage: One might propose that a set of natural numbers is decidable if there exists an effective procedure that always terminates, outputs 'yes' for members and 'no' for non-members, and runs in a number of steps bounded by a polynomial in the input. The first two conditions express the standard notion of decidability, whilst the third adds a constraint on computational efficiency. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis concerns the set of natural numbers encoding valid proofs in first-order arithmetic. Given any number n, one may effectively check whether it encodes a valid proof by verifying each step against the axioms and rules of inference. This procedure terminates for every input and gives the correct answer, so conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. The set is decidable by every standard account. Yet the verification procedure may require a number of steps that grows exponentially with the length of the encoded proof, not polynomially. Condition (iii) therefore fails, and the definition would classify this decidable set as undecidable. One might observe that the definition has confused two distinct concepts: decidability, which requires only the existence of a terminating effective procedure, and tractability, which requires that the procedure run within a specified time bound. These notions are of very different character. Many decidable sets are not decidable in polynomial time, and the concept of decidability, as it has been understood since the foundational work on effective computability, makes no reference to the speed of the deciding procedure. It appears that condition (iii) introduces a complexity-theoretic requirement that is foreign to the concept the definition purports to analyse.

definition: A sequence of functions f_n converges uniformly to a function f on a set S if and only if (i) for each point x in S, f_n(x) converges to f(x), (ii) each f_n is continuous on S, and (iii) the limit function f is continuous on S.
conditions: For each point x in S, f_n(x) converges to f(x)
Each f_n is continuous on S
The limit function f is continuous on S
counterexample: Consider the sequence of functions f_n defined on the interval [0,1] by f_n(x) equals x raised to the power n. Each f_n is continuous on [0,1]. The pointwise limit f is the function that equals zero for x in [0,1) and equals one at x equal to one. This limit function is not continuous, so condition (iii) fails, and indeed the convergence is not uniform. However, the three conditions do not correctly characterise uniform convergence even when all three hold. Let g_n(x) equal x raised to the power n divided by n on [0,1]. Each g_n is continuous. The pointwise limit g is identically zero, which is continuous. Conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) are all satisfied. The convergence happens to be uniform in this case, but consider instead h_n(x) equal to n times x times (1 minus x squared) raised to the power n on [0,1]. Each h_n is continuous; the limit h is zero everywhere, which is continuous. Conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) hold. Yet the convergence is not uniform, for the supremum of the absolute value of h_n does not tend to zero.
missing_condition: The definition omits the essential quantitative requirement of uniform convergence: that the supremum over all x in S of the absolute difference between f_n(x) and f(x) must tend to zero. The three listed conditions describe only pointwise convergence of continuous functions to a continuous limit, which is strictly weaker than uniform convergence.
passage: One might characterise uniform convergence of a sequence of functions by requiring that each function in the sequence converge pointwise to the limit, that each function be continuous, and that the limit function be continuous. This characterisation might seem plausible, since uniform convergence is known to preserve continuity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed on the unit interval. Consider the sequence h_n defined by h_n(x) equal to n times x times the quantity one minus x squared, all raised to the n-th power. Each h_n is continuous on the interval from zero to one. For each fixed x in this interval, h_n(x) tends to zero as n tends to infinity. The pointwise limit is thus the zero function, which is continuous. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the convergence is not uniform: the maximum value of h_n on the interval does not tend to zero but instead remains bounded away from zero or indeed tends to infinity. The supremum of the absolute difference between h_n and the zero function does not diminish. One might observe that the definition has confused two distinct modes of convergence. Pointwise convergence demands only that, for each fixed point, the function values approach the limit; uniform convergence demands that the rate of approach be, in a precise sense, uniform across all points of the domain. The three conditions listed capture the former but not the latter. It appears that the essential content of uniform convergence lies in the requirement that the supremum of the absolute difference tend to zero, a quantitative condition that the proposed definition entirely omits.

definition: A natural number n greater than one is prime if and only if (i) n has no divisor other than one and itself, (ii) n is not expressible as a product of two natural numbers each greater than one, and (iii) n belongs to a sequence generated by the Sieve of Eratosthenes applied to the natural numbers.
conditions: n has no divisor other than one and itself
n is not expressible as a product of two natural numbers each greater than one
n belongs to a sequence generated by the Sieve of Eratosthenes applied to the natural numbers
counterexample: Consider the number one. It has no divisor other than one and itself (its only divisor is one, which is both one and itself). It is not expressible as a product of two natural numbers each greater than one. And in certain historical formulations of the Sieve, one was included in the initial list and, having no multiples to strike off beyond itself, survived the process. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet the modern consensus, grounded in the fundamental theorem of arithmetic, excludes one from the primes, precisely because its inclusion would destroy the uniqueness of prime factorisation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that n be strictly greater than one in a manner that cannot be circumvented by the self-referential character of the phrase 'other than one and itself' when applied to the number one, and more fundamentally, it fails to require that n's inclusion among the primes preserve the uniqueness of prime factorisation.
passage: One might propose that a natural number greater than one is prime provided it has no divisor other than one and itself, cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each exceeding one, and belongs to the sequence produced by the Sieve of Eratosthenes. These three conditions appear, at first glance, to be entirely equivalent formulations of the same notion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider the number one. The number one has no divisor other than one and itself, for its only divisor is one, and one is both 'one' and 'itself.' It is not expressible as a product of two natural numbers each greater than one. And in certain historical presentations of the Sieve, one was included in the initial enumeration and, having no proper multiples to eliminate, survived the sifting process. Each condition appears to be met. Nevertheless, the inclusion of one among the primes has been rejected by modern arithmetic, and for good reason: if one were prime, the fundamental theorem of arithmetic, which asserts that every natural number greater than one admits a unique factorisation into primes, would fail, since one could be inserted as a factor any number of times without altering the product. It appears that the definition, by relying upon the phrase 'other than one and itself,' introduces an ambiguity that the number one exploits, and more importantly, it neglects the deeper algebraic requirement that the class of primes be so defined as to secure the uniqueness of factorisation.

definition: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point c if and only if (i) f is defined at c, (ii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists, and (iii) for every sequence of points converging to c, the images under f converge to f(c).
conditions: f is defined at c
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists
For every sequence of points converging to c the images under f converge to f(c)
counterexample: Consider the function f defined on the real numbers by the rule that f(x) equals the sine of one over x when x is not zero, and f(0) equals zero. At c equals zero, f is defined, and for every sequence of points converging to zero, one may verify that the images converge to zero (since the sine function is bounded and one over x grows without bound). The limit of f(x) as x approaches zero exists and equals zero. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet this function, while continuous at the origin, oscillates infinitely often in every neighbourhood of zero. It is by no means certain that the definition captures the stronger property of uniform continuity near c, and a mathematician seeking to extend results that require uniform behaviour in a neighbourhood — such as the interchange of limits — would find the definition insufficient.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between mere pointwise continuity at c and the stronger condition of continuity in a neighbourhood of c or uniform continuity near c, so that a function satisfying all three conditions may still exhibit pathological oscillatory behaviour that undermines the applicability of theorems requiring more than pointwise continuity.
passage: One might define continuity of a real-valued function at a point c by requiring that the function be defined at c, that the limit as x approaches c exist, and that every sequence converging to c have its images converge to f(c). These three conditions are, in standard analysis, equivalent formulations of the same notion of pointwise continuity, and they suffice for many purposes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis for broader mathematical purposes may be drawn from the study of oscillatory functions. Let f be defined by the rule that f(x) equals the sine of one over x for x not equal to zero, and f(0) equals zero. At c equals zero, f is defined; the limit of f(x) as x approaches zero exists and equals zero, since the sine is bounded while one over x grows without bound; and for every sequence converging to zero, the images converge to zero. All three conditions are satisfied, and the function is indeed continuous at the origin in the pointwise sense. Nevertheless, f oscillates infinitely often in every neighbourhood of zero, and this oscillatory behaviour prevents the function from being uniformly continuous on any interval containing the origin. A mathematician who relies upon the proposed definition in contexts where uniform behaviour near c is required — for instance, in the interchange of limits or the term-by-term integration of sequences — will find that the definition, though correct as far as it goes, fails to capture the regularity needed for these stronger results.

definition: An infinite series of real numbers converges if and only if (i) the partial sums form a bounded sequence, (ii) the terms of the series tend to zero, and (iii) the partial sums do not oscillate without settling to a single value.
conditions: The partial sums form a bounded sequence
The terms of the series tend to zero
The partial sums do not oscillate without settling to a single value
counterexample: Consider the series whose terms are given by one over n for n equals one, two, three, and so on — the harmonic series. The terms tend to zero, and the partial sums increase monotonically (and thus do not oscillate). One might initially suppose the partial sums are bounded, since the terms shrink towards zero. Yet the partial sums of the harmonic series grow without bound, diverging to infinity. In this case condition (ii) and condition (iii) are satisfied, but condition (i) fails. Now consider a rearrangement of the alternating harmonic series (whose terms are plus or minus one over n in alternation). By Riemann's rearrangement theorem, the terms may be reordered so that the partial sums converge to any desired real number. The terms still tend to zero, the partial sums are bounded and do not oscillate, yet the sum obtained depends upon the order of summation. All three conditions appear satisfied for the rearranged series, yet the series does not converge absolutely and its sum is not uniquely determined by the terms alone.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require absolute convergence or to specify the ordering of terms, so that a conditionally convergent series can be rearranged to yield any sum whatsoever while still satisfying all three stated conditions.
passage: One might propose that an infinite series of real numbers converges when its partial sums are bounded, its terms tend to zero, and its partial sums do not oscillate without settling to a single value. These three conditions capture much of what we informally associate with convergence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the theory of conditionally convergent series. Consider the alternating harmonic series, whose terms are plus or minus one over n in alternation. This series converges, in the usual sense, to the natural logarithm of two. Its partial sums are bounded, its terms tend to zero, and its partial sums settle to a single limiting value. Now let us rearrange the terms of this series — taking, say, several positive terms followed by one negative term, in a systematic pattern — so that the partial sums of the rearranged series converge to some other real number entirely. By the celebrated theorem of Riemann, any conditionally convergent series may be so rearranged as to converge to any desired sum, or indeed to diverge. The rearranged series still has terms tending to zero, its partial sums are bounded and do not oscillate, yet its sum differs from that of the original. All three conditions appear to be satisfied by the rearranged series, yet the sum is not determined by the terms alone but by their order. The definition, by neglecting to require absolute convergence, permits the sum of a series to be an artefact of arrangement rather than an intrinsic property of the terms.

definition: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X cannot be expressed as the union of two non-empty disjoint open sets, (ii) every continuous function from X to a discrete two-point space is constant, and (iii) for any two points in X there exists a continuous path joining them.
conditions: X cannot be expressed as the union of two non-empty disjoint open sets
Every continuous function from X to a discrete two-point space is constant
For any two points in X there exists a continuous path joining them
counterexample: Consider the topologist's sine curve, defined as the closure in the plane of the set of points (x, sin(1/x)) for x greater than zero. This space cannot be expressed as the union of two non-empty disjoint open sets; it is connected. Every continuous function from this space to a discrete two-point space is constant. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet condition (iii) fails: there is no continuous path from any point on the segment at x equals zero to any point on the oscillating curve. The space is connected but not path-connected.
missing_condition: The definition conflates connectedness with path-connectedness by including condition (iii), which is strictly stronger. A space may be connected in the topological sense — satisfying (i) and (ii) — without admitting continuous paths between all pairs of points.
passage: One might define a connected topological space as one that cannot be expressed as the union of two non-empty disjoint open sets, for which every continuous function into a discrete two-point space is constant, and in which any two points may be joined by a continuous path. These three conditions might appear to express the same fundamental idea of topological unity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the study of pathological subsets of the plane. Consider the topologist's sine curve: the closure of the set of all points of the form (x, sin(1/x)) for x greater than zero. This set consists of a wildly oscillating curve approaching the segment from (0, -1) to (0, 1) on the vertical axis, together with that segment itself. The space cannot be partitioned into two non-empty disjoint open sets; it is connected in the topological sense. Every continuous function from this space to a two-point discrete space is constant. Conditions (i) and (ii) are thus satisfied. Nevertheless, condition (iii) fails: there is no continuous path from any point on the vertical segment to any point on the oscillating curve, for any such path would have to traverse infinitely many oscillations in finite time. The space is connected but not path-connected. It appears that the definition conflates two distinct notions by incorporating a path-connectedness requirement that is strictly stronger than topological connectedness, thereby excluding spaces that are genuinely connected in the sense captured by conditions (i) and (ii).

definition: A function f from the natural numbers to the natural numbers is effectively computable if and only if (i) there exists a finite set of instructions that, when followed mechanically, produces f(n) for any input n, (ii) the computation terminates in a finite number of steps for every input, and (iii) the instructions make no appeal to insight, intuition, or ingenuity on the part of the person carrying them out.
conditions: There exists a finite set of instructions that when followed mechanically produces f(n) for any input n
The computation terminates in a finite number of steps for every input
The instructions make no appeal to insight intuition or ingenuity
counterexample: Suppose we define f(n) to be one if the nth Turing machine halts on empty input, and zero otherwise. Now consider the following set of instructions: 'Simulate the nth Turing machine on empty input; if it halts, output one; if it does not halt, output zero.' These instructions are finite, mechanical, and require no insight or ingenuity — one simply simulates the machine step by step. The instructions produce f(n) for every input n, since the function is well-defined. And the value of f is determined in each case. Yet condition (ii) is subtly at issue: for those n where the machine does not halt, the simulation never terminates, and no finite number of steps will produce the output zero. The instructions appear to define f but cannot compute it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the procedure for determining whether to output zero or one must itself terminate in finite time for every input, as distinct from the function being well-defined. The existence of finite instructions that would produce the answer if they terminated is not sufficient; what is needed is a guarantee that the instructions do in fact terminate for every input.
passage: One might propose that a function from the natural numbers to the natural numbers is effectively computable when there exists a finite set of mechanical instructions producing the function's value for any input, the computation terminates in finitely many steps, and the instructions require no insight or ingenuity. This characterisation captures the intuitive idea of a mechanical procedure. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the theory of computation itself. Let f(n) equal one if the nth Turing machine halts on empty input, and zero otherwise. Consider the instructions: simulate the nth Turing machine on empty input; if it halts, output one; otherwise output zero. These instructions are finite, wholly mechanical, and demand no insight. They produce f(n) for every input, since the function is well-defined. One might suppose that condition (ii) is satisfied, since the function always takes a definite value. Yet for those inputs n where the machine does not halt, the simulation never terminates, and no finite number of steps will yield the answer zero. The instructions define the correct value but cannot reach it. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that a finite mechanical procedure exist and that computation terminate, does not adequately distinguish between the well-definedness of a function and the effective computability of that function. The mere existence of instructions that would produce the answer were they to terminate is not equivalent to possessing a procedure that actually does terminate for every input.

definition: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function from A to B that is injective, (ii) there exists a function from B to A that is injective, and (iii) the elements of A can be listed in a sequence that exhausts A.
conditions: There exists an injective function from A to B
There exists an injective function from B to A
The elements of A can be listed in a sequence that exhausts A
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers and the set of real numbers between zero and one. There exists an injective function from the natural numbers to the real numbers (simply send each natural number n to one over n plus one). There exists an injective function from the reals between zero and one to the natural numbers if we accept the axiom of choice and suitable encodings — but in fact, by Cantor's theorem, no such injection exists. Let us instead consider two uncountable sets: the set of real numbers and the set of points in the plane. There exist injections in both directions, satisfying conditions (i) and (ii). By the Cantor-Bernstein theorem, these sets have the same cardinality. Yet condition (iii) fails for both, since neither set can be listed in a sequence. The definition excludes uncountable sets from having well-defined cardinality comparisons, even though the Cantor-Bernstein theorem establishes their equicardinality.
missing_condition: The definition improperly includes condition (iii), which restricts the analysis to countable sets, thereby excluding uncountable sets from cardinality comparisons despite the fact that the Cantor-Bernstein theorem guarantees equicardinality whenever mutual injections exist, regardless of countability.
passage: One might propose that two sets have the same cardinality when there exist injective functions in both directions and the elements of the first set can be enumerated in a sequence. The first two conditions capture the essential content of the Cantor-Bernstein theorem, which asserts that mutual injections suffice to establish equicardinality. Yet the third condition introduces a restriction that tells against the generality of the analysis. Consider the set of real numbers and the set of points in the Euclidean plane. There exist injective functions in both directions — one may, for instance, interleave the decimal expansions of coordinates — so that conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, and the Cantor-Bernstein theorem establishes that the two sets have the same cardinality. Yet neither set can be listed in a sequence that exhausts its elements; both are uncountable. Condition (iii) fails for both sets. The definition, by requiring enumerability, excludes from its scope the very sets for which cardinality comparisons are most interesting and most necessary. It appears that the proposed analysis conflates the notion of equicardinality, which is perfectly well-defined for sets of any size, with the special property of countability, which pertains only to sets whose elements admit of sequential enumeration.

definition: A formal system S is complete if and only if (i) every well-formed formula of S or its negation is provable within S, (ii) S contains the axioms of basic arithmetic, and (iii) S is consistent, in the sense that no contradiction is provable within S.
conditions: Every well-formed formula or its negation is provable within S
S contains the axioms of basic arithmetic
S is consistent
