counterexample: Let us suppose, for the sake of argument, that such a system S exists: it proves, for every well-formed formula, either the formula or its negation; it contains the axioms of basic arithmetic; and it is consistent. All three conditions are jointly satisfied. Yet by the first incompleteness theorem of Goedel, no consistent formal system that contains basic arithmetic can prove every arithmetical truth or its negation. The three conditions cannot, in fact, be jointly satisfied by any system. The definition characterises an object that does not and cannot exist.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that its three conditions are jointly unsatisfiable for any system containing basic arithmetic, owing to the incompleteness theorems. It conflates the ideal of completeness with the requirements of consistency and arithmetical expressiveness, which are provably incompatible.
passage: One might define a complete formal system as one in which every well-formed formula or its negation is provable, which contains the axioms of basic arithmetic, and which is consistent. This characterisation expresses the mathematician's ideal of a system that settles every question within its language. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis — indeed, which reveals its impossibility — may be drawn from the celebrated results of Goedel. Suppose, for the sake of argument, that a system S satisfies all three conditions: it is complete in the sense that every formula or its negation is provable; it contains basic arithmetic; and it is free from contradiction. All three conditions are jointly met. Yet the first incompleteness theorem demonstrates that no consistent system containing basic arithmetic can decide every arithmetical formula. The conditions cannot be simultaneously satisfied by any actual formal system. The definition, by requiring completeness, arithmetic, and consistency in a single system, characterises an object that is provably non-existent. It appears that the proposed analysis conflates the desiderata of completeness with the constraints imposed by the nature of formal arithmetic, failing to acknowledge that these desiderata are, in combination, unrealisable.

definition: A set of natural numbers D is decidable if and only if (i) for every natural number n there exists a procedure that determines whether n belongs to D, (ii) the procedure always gives a definite yes-or-no answer, and (iii) the procedure can be described in a finite number of words.
conditions: For every natural number n there exists a procedure that determines whether n belongs to D
The procedure always gives a definite yes-or-no answer
The procedure can be described in a finite number of words
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers n such that n encodes a Turing machine that halts on empty input. For every n, there exists the following procedure, described in finitely many words: 'Simulate the nth Turing machine on empty input; if it halts, answer yes; if it runs forever, answer no.' This procedure gives a definite yes-or-no answer for every n in principle, since each machine either halts or does not. And the procedure is described in a finite number of words. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet the halting set is undecidable: no effective procedure can carry out this determination in finite time for every input.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the procedure terminate in a finite number of steps for every input, so that a procedure which gives definite answers 'in principle' but may run forever on some inputs satisfies all three conditions without rendering the set genuinely decidable.
passage: One might propose that a set of natural numbers is decidable when, for every number, there exists a finitely describable procedure that delivers a definite yes-or-no answer as to membership. This characterisation captures the intuition that decidability involves a clear-cut method of determination. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the theory of computation. Consider the set of those natural numbers that encode Turing machines which halt on empty input. For any such number, one may describe, in finitely many words, the following procedure: simulate the machine on empty input; if it halts, answer yes; if it runs forever, answer no. This procedure gives a definite answer for every input in principle, for every Turing machine either halts or does not, and the description of the procedure is manifestly finite. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Yet the set in question is the halting set, which is well known to be undecidable. The difficulty is that the procedure, while finitely described and definite in its intended output, may fail to terminate on those inputs for which the answer is no. The definition, by requiring only a finite description and a definite answer, fails to require that the procedure actually reach its answer in a finite number of steps for every input. Without this requirement, the distinction between decidability and mere well-definedness collapses.

definition: A problem P is solvable by an algorithm if and only if (i) there exists a Turing machine that accepts every instance of P that has a positive answer, (ii) the Turing machine rejects every instance that has a negative answer, and (iii) the Turing machine operates within a bounded amount of memory for each input.
conditions: There exists a Turing machine that accepts every positive instance of P
The Turing machine rejects every negative instance of P
The Turing machine operates within a bounded amount of memory for each input
counterexample: Consider the problem of determining whether a given context-free grammar generates all possible strings over its alphabet — the universality problem for context-free grammars. A Turing machine can be constructed that accepts every positive instance and rejects every negative instance, for this problem is known to be decidable. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet the machine may require an amount of memory that grows without bound as the size of the input grammar increases. Condition (iii) fails. Under the proposed definition, this decidable problem would not count as solvable by an algorithm, even though a perfectly effective procedure exists for it.
missing_condition: The definition improperly includes a bounded-memory condition that conflates algorithmic solvability with bounded resource usage, thereby excluding decidable problems whose solutions require memory proportional to the size of the input from the class of algorithmically solvable problems.
passage: One might propose that a problem is solvable by an algorithm when there exists a Turing machine that accepts every positive instance, rejects every negative instance, and operates within a bounded amount of memory. The first two conditions capture the essential requirement of effective decidability. Yet the third condition introduces a resource constraint that tells against the generality of the analysis. Consider the problem of determining whether a given context-free grammar generates every possible string over its alphabet. This problem is decidable: a Turing machine can be constructed that accepts every positive instance and rejects every negative one, satisfying conditions (i) and (ii). Yet the memory required by such a machine may grow without bound as the input grammar increases in complexity; there is no fixed bound on the memory consumed. Condition (iii) fails. Under the proposed definition, this decidable problem would be excluded from the class of algorithmically solvable problems, even though an effective procedure exists that correctly decides every instance. It appears that the definition conflates the notion of algorithmic solvability with the imposition of fixed resource bounds, thereby excluding problems that are perfectly decidable but whose solutions require computational resources that scale with the size of the input.

definition: A function f from a metric space X to a metric space Y is uniformly continuous if and only if (i) f is continuous at every point of X, (ii) X is a bounded set, and (iii) f maps Cauchy sequences in X to Cauchy sequences in Y.
conditions: f is continuous at every point of X
X is a bounded set
f maps Cauchy sequences in X to Cauchy sequences in Y
counterexample: Consider the function f(x) equals x squared defined on the open interval (0, 1). The interval is bounded, f is continuous at every point, and since (0, 1) is a subset of the real line, f maps Cauchy sequences to Cauchy sequences (the image of a Cauchy sequence under a continuous function on a bounded interval in the reals remains Cauchy). All three conditions appear satisfied. Now consider f(x) equals x squared on the entire real line. The function is continuous at every point and maps Cauchy sequences to Cauchy sequences. Yet the real line is unbounded, and f is not uniformly continuous on it: for large x, a fixed small change in x produces an arbitrarily large change in x squared. Condition (ii) fails. But boundedness of the domain does not by itself guarantee uniform continuity; consider f(x) equals sin(1/x) on the open interval (0, 1). This function is continuous at every point, the domain is bounded, and yet f is not uniformly continuous because it oscillates arbitrarily rapidly near zero.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the domain be compact (that is, closed and bounded in the case of subsets of the real line), rather than merely bounded. By the Heine-Cantor theorem, continuity on a compact set entails uniform continuity, but continuity on a set that is bounded but not closed does not.
passage: One might propose that a function between metric spaces is uniformly continuous when it is continuous at every point, the domain is bounded, and Cauchy sequences are preserved. These conditions capture the intuitions of pointwise regularity, spatial confinement, and sequential preservation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the behaviour of functions on bounded but non-compact domains. Consider f(x) equals sin(1/x) defined on the open interval (0, 1). The function is continuous at every point of its domain, and the domain is bounded. Moreover, the function maps Cauchy sequences in (0, 1) to sequences in the bounded set [-1, 1]. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet f is not uniformly continuous on (0, 1): as x approaches zero, the function oscillates with ever-increasing rapidity, so that for any fixed tolerance in the codomain there is no single threshold in the domain that works uniformly across all points. The difficulty lies in the fact that the domain, while bounded, is not closed, and hence not compact. By the Heine-Cantor theorem, a continuous function on a compact metric space is uniformly continuous, but no such guarantee attaches to continuity on a merely bounded set. It appears that the definition, by requiring only boundedness of the domain, neglects the essential role of compactness in securing uniform continuity.

definition: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n has no positive divisors other than one and itself, and (iii) n is expressible as the sum of two squares.
conditions: n is greater than one
n has no positive divisors other than one and itself
n is expressible as the sum of two squares
counterexample: Consider the number three. It is greater than one and has no positive divisors other than one and itself, thus satisfying conditions (i) and (ii). However, three cannot be expressed as the sum of two perfect squares, for the only squares less than or equal to three are zero and one, and no combination of these yields three. Three is indisputably prime, yet it fails condition (iii), revealing that this condition is not necessary for primality.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is spurious: expressibility as the sum of two squares is not a necessary property of primes, as demonstrated by primes congruent to three modulo four.
passage: It is a commonplace of arithmetic that the prime numbers are those integers greater than unity which admit of no divisors save unity and themselves. To this familiar characterisation one might be tempted to append the further requirement that the number be expressible as the sum of two squares, a property enjoyed by many primes. One might note, for instance, that five equals four plus one, thirteen equals nine plus four, and so forth, and be led thereby to suppose that this expressibility is part of what it means for a number to be prime. Yet this supposition is swiftly overturned. The number three satisfies the first two conditions admirably: it exceeds unity and possesses no divisors other than one and itself. It is, by universal agreement, prime. Yet three cannot be written as the sum of two squares, for the only perfect squares not exceeding it are zero and one, and no pairing of these produces three. We are thus compelled to recognise that the third condition is not necessary for primality. The analysis, in appending it, conflates a property that happens to belong to certain primes with one that belongs to all. An adequate characterisation of primality must confine itself to divisibility conditions and resist the temptation to incorporate properties that, however interesting, are merely contingently associated with the prime numbers.

definition: A real-valued function f is continuous at a point c if and only if (i) f is defined at c, (ii) the limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists, (iii) the limit equals f(c), and (iv) f is differentiable at c.
conditions: f is defined at c
The limit of f(x) as x approaches c exists
The limit equals f(c)
f is differentiable at c
counterexample: Consider the function f(x) equal to the absolute value of x at the point c equal to zero. The function is defined at zero, where it takes the value zero. The limit of the absolute value of x as x approaches zero exists and equals zero, which equals f(zero). Thus conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) are satisfied. Yet the function is not differentiable at zero, for the left-hand derivative equals negative one whilst the right-hand derivative equals positive one. The function is nevertheless continuous at zero by any standard reckoning, so condition (iv) is not necessary for continuity.
missing_condition: Condition (iv) is not necessary for continuity; differentiability is a strictly stronger property that excludes many functions universally regarded as continuous.
passage: The standard analysis of continuity at a point, as it has developed since the work of Cauchy and Weierstrass, requires three conditions: that the function be defined at the point, that the limit exist, and that the limit coincide with the value of the function. To these one might be tempted to add the further stipulation that the function be differentiable at the point in question, reasoning perhaps that a truly well-behaved function ought to possess a tangent at every point where it is continuous. Yet this addition proves too demanding. Let us consider the absolute value function at the origin. This function is defined at zero, its limit as the argument tends to zero exists and equals zero, and this limit agrees with the function's value there. By the classical criteria, the function is continuous at zero, and indeed it is so regarded in every textbook of analysis. Yet it fails to be differentiable at zero, for the slopes of the secant lines approach different values from the left and from the right. We are thus compelled to recognise that differentiability, though it implies continuity, is not implied by it. The proposed fourth condition excludes from the class of continuous functions many that plainly deserve membership, and the analysis is thereby rendered too restrictive.

definition: An infinite series converges if and only if (i) the partial sums form a bounded sequence, (ii) the terms of the series tend to zero, and (iii) the terms are eventually monotonically decreasing in absolute value.
conditions: The partial sums form a bounded sequence
The terms of the series tend to zero
The terms are eventually monotonically decreasing in absolute value
counterexample: Consider the series whose terms alternate in sign and decrease in magnitude but are grouped so that each positive term is followed by two negative terms of half its size: one, negative one half, negative one quarter, one eighth, negative one sixteenth, negative one thirty-second, and so on. The partial sums are bounded, the terms tend to zero, but the terms are not eventually monotonically decreasing in absolute value, since positive terms exceeding their immediate predecessors recur at regular intervals. Yet the series converges, for the grouped partial sums form a Cauchy sequence. Condition (iii) is therefore not necessary for convergence.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is not necessary; many convergent series have terms whose absolute values fail to decrease monotonically, and the Cauchy criterion provides the correct necessary and sufficient condition.
passage: It is natural to seek simple conditions under which an infinite series may be said to converge. Boundedness of the partial sums and the vanishing of the terms are conditions that recommend themselves on intuitive grounds, and to these one might add the further requirement that the terms eventually decrease monotonically in absolute value, a condition reminiscent of the hypotheses of the alternating series test. Yet this triple of conditions does not correctly delineate the class of convergent series. One may construct a series whose terms tend to zero, whose partial sums remain bounded, and which does in fact converge, yet whose terms fail to decrease monotonically in absolute value at any stage. Suppose we arrange terms in groups: a positive term followed by two negative terms each half its magnitude, with the pattern repeating at ever smaller scales. The partial sums fluctuate but remain bounded and indeed converge, yet the absolute values of the terms increase at each transition from a negative to a positive group. We are thus led to conclude that monotonic decrease of the terms in absolute value, whilst sufficient in conjunction with alternating signs, is not a necessary feature of convergent series. The analysis confuses a useful sufficient condition with a necessary one and must be revised accordingly.

definition: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets, (ii) X is path-connected, and (iii) every continuous real-valued function on X has the intermediate value property.
conditions: X cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets
X is path-connected
Every continuous real-valued function on X has the intermediate value property
counterexample: Consider the topologist's sine curve, defined as the union of the graph of sin(1/x) for x greater than zero and the segment of the y-axis from negative one to one. This space cannot be partitioned into two disjoint non-empty open sets and is therefore connected in the standard sense. Every continuous real-valued function on it enjoys the intermediate value property. Yet the space is not path-connected, for there is no continuous path from a point on the y-axis segment to a point on the oscillating curve. Condition (ii) fails, but the space is connected, so path-connectedness is not necessary.
missing_condition: Condition (ii) is not necessary for connectedness; path-connectedness is a strictly stronger property, and the classical definition requires only condition (i).
passage: The notion of connectedness is among the most fundamental in topology, and it is tempting to characterise it by combining several properties that connected spaces frequently enjoy. One might propose that a space is connected just in case it admits no partition into disjoint non-empty open sets, is path-connected, and has the intermediate value property for continuous real-valued functions. These conditions are indeed satisfied by many familiar connected spaces, such as intervals of the real line. Yet let us consider the so-called topologist's sine curve, a space formed by adjoining the closed segment from negative one to one on the vertical axis to the graph of the sine of the reciprocal of x for positive x. This space is connected in the standard topological sense: no partition into two disjoint non-empty open sets is possible, and every continuous real-valued function on it possesses the intermediate value property. Nevertheless, it is not path-connected, for the wild oscillation of the curve near the origin prevents the construction of a continuous path joining a point on the vertical segment to a point on the curve. We must therefore recognise that path-connectedness, whilst it implies connectedness, is not implied by it, and the proposed analysis is overly restrictive.

definition: A function from natural numbers to natural numbers is computable if and only if (i) there exists a finite procedure that calculates f(n) for every input n, (ii) the procedure halts on every input, and (iii) the procedure can be expressed using only primitive recursive operations.
conditions: There exists a finite procedure that calculates f(n) for every input n
The procedure halts on every input
The procedure can be expressed using only primitive recursive operations
counterexample: Consider the Ackermann function, which is defined by a finite procedure, halts on every pair of natural number inputs, and is therefore computable in the standard sense. Yet the Ackermann function is not primitive recursive: it grows faster than any primitive recursive function and cannot be expressed using only the operations of primitive recursion. Condition (iii) is satisfied by neither the Ackermann function nor any function that grows as rapidly, yet these functions are computable. The third condition is therefore too restrictive.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is too restrictive; computability requires only that the function be calculable by some effective procedure that halts, not that it be expressible through primitive recursive operations alone.
passage: The notion of a computable function has received extensive analysis, and the Church-Turing thesis asserts that it coincides with the class of functions calculable by Turing machines. One might attempt a more elementary characterisation by requiring that a computable function admit a finite halting procedure expressible solely in terms of primitive recursive operations — composition, primitive recursion, and the initial functions. This analysis captures a wide class of functions, including addition, multiplication, and exponentiation. Yet it proves too narrow. The Ackermann function provides a decisive case. This function is defined by a perfectly finite set of recursive equations and is known to halt on every input of natural numbers. By any reasonable standard, it is computable. Yet it outgrows every primitive recursive function and cannot be defined by primitive recursive means alone; its definition requires unbounded search or an equivalent device. We are thus led to conclude that the restriction to primitive recursive operations excludes genuinely computable functions from the class. An adequate analysis must permit the use of minimisation or some analogous operation, on pain of failing to capture functions that effective procedures can manifestly calculate.

definition: Two sets have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function from one set to the other, (ii) the function is injective, and (iii) both sets are finite.
conditions: There exists a function from one set to the other
The function is injective
Both sets are finite
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers and the set of even natural numbers. The function that sends each natural number n to 2n is an injection from the natural numbers to the even natural numbers, and its inverse is also well defined, establishing a bijection. These two sets have the same cardinality in the standard Cantorian sense. Yet neither set is finite, so condition (iii) fails. The analysis excludes the possibility of comparing infinite cardinalities, which is precisely the context in which the concept is most illuminating.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is spurious and must be removed; the concept of equicardinality applies to infinite sets as well, and the correct requirement is that the function be a bijection (both injective and surjective), not that the sets be finite.
passage: The comparison of the sizes of sets is one of the most fruitful developments in the foundations of mathematics, and it might seem that a straightforward analysis in terms of injective functions between sets would suffice. One might add the restriction to finite sets, reasoning that the notion of size is most intuitive in the finite case. Yet this restriction vitiates the analysis entirely. Consider the natural numbers and the even natural numbers. The mapping that assigns to each natural number n the number 2n is plainly injective, and its inverse, which assigns to each even number m the number m divided by two, is equally well defined. A bijection exists, and in the standard sense these two sets have the same cardinality. Yet neither set is finite, and the proposed analysis, by requiring finiteness, pronounces them incomparable. One might observe that it is precisely in the comparison of infinite collections that the concept of cardinality does its most important work; to confine the analysis to finite sets is to exclude the very cases that motivated its development. We must therefore recognise that the finiteness condition is not merely unnecessary but positively obstructive, and that an adequate account must permit the comparison of sets of any size, requiring only that the function between them be bijective.

definition: A metric space is complete if and only if (i) every Cauchy sequence in the space converges to a limit in the space, (ii) the space is bounded, and (iii) the space is closed as a subset of some ambient space.
conditions: Every Cauchy sequence in the space converges to a limit in the space
The space is bounded
The space is closed as a subset of some ambient space
counterexample: Consider the real line with the standard metric. Every Cauchy sequence of real numbers converges to a real number, so the real line is complete. Yet the real line is not bounded, for it extends without limit in both directions. Condition (ii) fails, but completeness holds. The analysis incorrectly requires boundedness, which is an independent property having no necessary connection to the convergence of Cauchy sequences.
missing_condition: Condition (ii) is spurious; boundedness is not necessary for completeness, as the real line itself demonstrates.
passage: Completeness is a property of central importance in analysis, ensuring that limits of Cauchy sequences remain within the space. One might propose that completeness requires not only the convergence of Cauchy sequences but also that the space be bounded and closed. The intuition behind including boundedness might rest on the observation that many familiar complete spaces, such as closed intervals of the real line, are indeed bounded. Yet this inclusion proves unjustified. The real line itself, equipped with the standard Euclidean metric, is the paradigmatic complete metric space: every Cauchy sequence of real numbers converges to a real number. The real line is, however, manifestly unbounded; it extends without limit in both the positive and negative directions. Condition (ii) of the proposed analysis is not satisfied, yet the space is complete by any standard. We are thus compelled to remove the boundedness condition from the analysis. An adequate characterisation of completeness requires only that every Cauchy sequence converge to a limit within the space; the question of whether the space is bounded is an entirely separate matter that has no bearing on the convergence properties of Cauchy sequences.

definition: A set of sentences in a formal language is decidable if and only if (i) there exists an algorithm that, given any sentence, determines in finite time whether the sentence belongs to the set, (ii) the algorithm is deterministic, and (iii) the formal language has a finite alphabet.
conditions: There exists an algorithm that, given any sentence, determines in finite time whether the sentence belongs to the set
The algorithm is deterministic
The formal language has a finite alphabet
counterexample: Consider the set of valid sentences of propositional logic over a countably infinite set of proposition letters. Although the alphabet of proposition letters is infinite, one can decide validity by truth-table methods applied to the finitely many letters occurring in any given formula. A deterministic algorithm exists that resolves membership in finite time. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, condition (iii) fails, yet the set is decidable. The finiteness of the alphabet is not necessary for decidability.
missing_condition: Condition (iii) is unnecessary; decidability depends on the existence of an effective decision procedure for the set, not on the size of the underlying alphabet, provided that sentences themselves are finite strings.
passage: The notion of decidability occupies a pivotal position in mathematical logic, marking the boundary between problems that admit algorithmic resolution and those that do not. A natural analysis might require three things: a deterministic algorithm that resolves membership in finite time, and the additional proviso that the formal language employ a finite alphabet. The third condition might seem harmless, even obvious, inasmuch as most familiar formal languages do have finite alphabets. Yet it is not necessary. Consider propositional logic with a countably infinite stock of proposition letters. Any given formula contains only finitely many letters, and the standard truth-table method decides validity by examining all possible truth-value assignments to those letters. The algorithm is deterministic and terminates in finite time, and the set of valid formulae is therefore decidable. Yet the alphabet, comprising infinitely many proposition letters, is not finite. We must recognise that decidability pertains to the existence of an effective procedure for resolving membership, and that the cardinality of the alphabet is beside the point so long as individual sentences remain finite objects. The third condition, while satisfied in many typical cases, is not a necessary ingredient of decidability.

definition: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n is odd, and (iii) n is not divisible by any prime smaller than itself.
conditions: n is greater than one
n is odd
n is not divisible by any prime smaller than itself
counterexample: Consider the number two. It is greater than one and is not divisible by any prime smaller than itself, since the only natural number smaller than two that exceeds one is nonexistent. Conditions (i) and (iii) are therefore satisfied. Yet two is even, and condition (ii) accordingly fails. Two is, however, universally recognised as prime — indeed, it is the smallest prime. The requirement of oddness is therefore not necessary for primality.
missing_condition: Condition (ii) is not necessary; the number two is the sole even prime, and an adequate analysis of primality must not exclude it by requiring oddness.
passage: The prime numbers have been the subject of investigation since the earliest days of mathematics, and it might seem natural to characterise them as those numbers exceeding unity that are odd and divisible by no smaller prime. The requirement of oddness may appear innocuous, given that the overwhelming majority of primes are indeed odd, and one might be tempted to regard it as a harmless addition to the analysis. Yet it proves fatal to the adequacy of the characterisation. The number two is greater than one and possesses no prime divisor smaller than itself, for no prime less than two exists. It satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but fails condition (ii), being even. Yet two is prime by every standard account; it is the unique even prime. We are thus compelled to recognise that the inclusion of oddness as a necessary condition excludes from the class of primes a number that indisputably belongs to it. An adequate analysis must restrict itself to conditions that are genuinely necessary, and oddness, however typical of primes, is not among them. The case of two serves as a salutary reminder that properties shared by nearly all members of a class are not thereby necessary conditions of membership.
